subtopic_detail: Intussusception - imaging-confirmed without peritonism
stem:
An 11-month-old previously well boy is brought to the metropolitan emergency department with a 14 hour history of intermittent severe abdominal pain. The mother describes that he suddenly starts crying, draws his legs up to his tummy, then settles drowsily between episodes. He has had two non-bilious vomits and one dark slimy nappy this morning.

On examination he is quiet and pale between episodes but alert. The abdomen is soft with a long thin lump palpable in the right upper area; bowel sounds present. He has not had any recent gastroenteritis or weight loss, and there is no abdominal distension or guarding. Capillary refill is 2 seconds and peripheries are warm.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138/min, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, palpable elongated mass right upper quadrant; no peritonism, no guarding, normal bowel sounds; nappy contains dark mucoid stool
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pH 7.36, lactate 1.4 mmol/L (0.5-2.2); FBC and electrolytes within normal limits; abdominal ultrasound shows target sign in right upper quadrant with ileocolic intussusception and no free fluid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Intravenous fluid resuscitation, paediatric surgical consultation, and image-guided air enema reduction by an interventional radiologist
options.rationale: Ileocolic intussusception without peritonitis or shock is reduced first by air or contrast enema under fluoroscopic guidance with surgical backup; success rates are 80-90% in this scenario. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Conservative bowel rest with intravenous fluids, nasogastric decompression, and clinical reassessment within the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Delay risks bowel ischaemia, perforation, and increased need for surgical resection; image-guided reduction is time critical once intussusception is confirmed without peritonism. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Urgent open laparotomy and primary manual surgical reduction in the operating theatre overnight by the on-call paediatric surgical team
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed enema reduction, peritonitis, shock, or evidence of perforation; first-line is non-operative enema reduction in stable children. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Outpatient paediatric gastroenterology referral for colonoscopy within the next 2 weeks for any further clinical evaluation
options.rationale: Colonoscopy has no role in acute intussusception diagnosis or reduction; ultrasound confirms diagnosis and enema reduces. Delay risks bowel necrosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Trial of oral rehydration solution with reassessment of clinical status in the paediatric short stay unit after 4 hours
options.rationale: Oral rehydration delays definitive reduction in confirmed intussusception; without enema or surgical reduction the obstruction progresses to ischaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Intussusception classically affects infants 3 months to 3 years and presents with paroxysmal pain, drawing up of legs, lethargy between episodes, and (later) blood and mucus per rectum (dark slimy stool). Confirmed by ultrasound showing the target or doughnut sign. In a clinically stable child without peritonism or shock, image-guided air or contrast enema reduction by interventional radiology with surgical backup is the standard first-line therapy.
key_points: Air enema first-line if no peritonism, shock, or perforation; 80-90% success.
Surgery reserved for failed enema reduction, peritonism, shock, or perforation.
Ultrasound is the diagnostic test of choice (target/pseudokidney sign).
Recurrence after successful enema reduction is 10-15%; explain to parents.
Pathological lead point (Meckel's, polyp, lymphoma) is more likely if age <3 months or >3 years or recurrent.
explanation.pearls: Air enema reduction in ileocolic intussusception under fluoroscopy has 80-90% success with perforation rate <1%; book surgical backup but do not skip to laparotomy without trying it first.

subtopic_detail: Pyloric stenosis - correcting electrolytes before surgery
stem:
A 5-week-old boy, born at term and previously thriving, is brought to the paediatric assessment unit with a 10 day history of progressively worsening vomiting after feeds. The mother describes vomiting as forceful, the milk shooting out across the cot, and the baby seems immediately hungry to feed again. The vomit has never contained any green colour.

His current weight is 7% below his weight at the 4 week health check. On examination he appears mildly dehydrated with sunken anterior fontanelle and dry mucous membranes. There is visible peristalsis across the upper abdomen; a small olive-like mass is palpable in the epigastrium during a test feed.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 162/min, BP 82/50 mmHg, RR 38/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mildly dehydrated; visible peristaltic waves left to right across upper abdomen; firm olive-like mass palpable just lateral to right of epigastrium; normal nappy output today
data_table.Investigations: Na 132 mmol/L (135-145), K 3.0 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), Cl 88 mmol/L (98-107), HCO3 32 mmol/L (22-26), urea 7.5 mmol/L (2.5-7.0); pH 7.50; ultrasound pylorus 5 mm muscle thickness 18 mm length

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Transfer immediately to the operating theatre for pyloromyotomy within the next hour to relieve the obstruction
options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is never a surgical emergency; operating on a child with hypochloraemic hypokalaemic alkalosis risks post-operative apnoea from persistent metabolic alkalosis depressing respiratory drive. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride with potassium replacement until chloride above 100 mmol/L and bicarbonate below 30 mmol/L, then surgery
options.rationale: Correction of hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis to chloride >100 and bicarbonate <30 with adequate urine output is the RCH and AAP criteria before pyloromyotomy to prevent post-operative apnoea. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Continue oral feeds with thickened formula at smaller volumes and review the response to feeding over the next 48 hours
options.rationale: Mechanical gastric outlet obstruction will not respond to thickening; ongoing vomiting will worsen dehydration and alkalosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

options.text: Trial of oral erythromycin 10 mg/kg three times daily to improve gastric emptying and prevent need for surgery in this infant
options.rationale: Erythromycin has no role in pyloric stenosis (a fixed muscle hypertrophy); surgical pyloromyotomy is curative. Erythromycin in infants is also associated with hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: Order an upper gastrointestinal contrast study to confirm diagnosis and exclude malrotation before any surgical intervention
options.rationale: Ultrasound has already confirmed pyloric stenosis (muscle thickness >=3-4 mm, length >=14-17 mm); contrast study is unnecessary and delays correction of electrolytes. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric stenosis)

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis presents at 3-6 weeks with non-bilious projectile vomiting, hungry feeds, and hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Ultrasound confirms with pyloric muscle thickness >=3-4 mm and length >=14-17 mm. Pyloromyotomy is curative but is NEVER an emergency; electrolyte correction first, with chloride >100, bicarbonate <30, and urine output established, prevents post-operative apnoea from persistent alkalosis blunting respiratory drive.
key_points: Diagnostic ultrasound criteria: pyloric muscle thickness >=3-4 mm, length >=14-17 mm.
Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis is the classic biochemistry.
Correct fluid/electrolytes BEFORE surgery to prevent post-op apnoea.
Target chloride >100 mmol/L and bicarbonate <30 mmol/L before theatre.
Erythromycin in early infancy is itself a risk factor for pyloric stenosis.
explanation.pearls: Pyloric stenosis is a medical preparation, then a surgical fix - never a 1am operation. Chloride >100 and bicarbonate <30 are the gates.

subtopic_detail: Neonatal jaundice - phototherapy thresholds with negative coombs
stem:
A 5-day-old male infant born at 39+2 weeks via uncomplicated normal vaginal birth is reviewed in the neonatal home visit clinic at 108 hours of life. He is exclusively breastfed but feeding has been slow. The midwife reports him as visibly yellow to the mid-chest. His mother is blood group O Rhesus positive and the infant is A Rhesus positive.

On examination he is alert, well perfused, with normal tone and reflexes. There is no hepatosplenomegaly or bruising. Birth weight was 3450 g and current weight is 3210 g (7% loss). His direct antiglobulin test is negative and there are no spherocytes on film. A transcutaneous bilirubin reading of 320 micromol/L is confirmed by serum total bilirubin 332 micromol/L with conjugated fraction <5%.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138/min, BP 72/45 mmHg, RR 44/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert and active; jaundice to mid-chest; no hepatosplenomegaly, bruising or cephalohaematoma; normal tone; weight 3210 g (7% below birth weight)
data_table.Investigations: Serum total bilirubin 332 micromol/L, conjugated 12 micromol/L; haemoglobin 165 g/L (140-200); reticulocyte count 3.0% (1-2); direct antiglobulin test negative; blood group A positive (mother O positive); peripheral film no spherocytes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the parents that this represents physiological jaundice; encourage breastfeeding and arrange community midwife review in 48 hours
options.rationale: The serum bilirubin of 332 micromol/L at 108 hours of life crosses the RCH phototherapy line for a term infant without risk factors; deferring treatment risks ongoing rise and acute bilirubin encephalopathy. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Replace all breastfeeds with formula for 48 hours and recheck serum bilirubin then with a view to restarting breastfeeding once it falls
options.rationale: Brief formula supplementation can be considered as an adjunct in dehydration-related jaundice but is not the primary treatment when bilirubin crosses the phototherapy line; phototherapy is required regardless. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Commence intensive phototherapy and recheck serum bilirubin in 4-6 hours with ongoing breastfeeding and lactation support
options.rationale: Bilirubin 332 micromol/L at 108 hours in a well term infant without haemolysis exceeds the RCH phototherapy threshold; intensive phototherapy with continued breastfeeding plus lactation support is the AU standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Arrange immediate exchange transfusion under neonatology supervision given the rapidly rising bilirubin level threatening kernicterus risk
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion threshold at 108 hours in a low-risk term infant is approximately >425 micromol/L, or failure of intensive phototherapy; this infant has not reached that line. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

options.text: Send thyroid function tests, urine culture, and G6PD assay before any treatment is started and await results overnight before intervening
options.rationale: Workup for prolonged or pathological jaundice (conjugated, age >14 days, or rising after phototherapy) does not replace immediate phototherapy when the unconjugated bilirubin threshold is crossed. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

explanation.summary: Neonatal hyperbilirubinaemia is plotted against age-in-hours on the RCH bilirubin nomogram; the threshold for phototherapy is a function of age, gestation, and risk factors (haemolysis, sepsis, prematurity). A negative DAT and matched maternal-infant ABO blood groups exclude haemolytic disease; this infant has breastfeeding-associated jaundice from inadequate feeding and weight loss, but the bilirubin level itself dictates phototherapy. Continued breastfeeding with lactation support is the standard adjunct rather than formula switching.
key_points: Treatment threshold depends on age in hours plotted on the RCH nomogram.
Negative DAT plus normal reticulocytes excludes immune haemolysis.
Breastfeeding-associated jaundice from inadequate intake is treated with phototherapy plus continued breastfeeding and lactation support.
Exchange transfusion threshold is approximately 425 micromol/L at this age in low-risk infants.
Phototherapy is intensive (multiple lights, maximised skin exposure) when above treatment line.
explanation.pearls: When DAT is negative and ABO/Rh are matched, haemolytic disease is excluded - but the absolute bilirubin level on the RCH nomogram is what determines whether phototherapy starts now, not the underlying mechanism.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy

subtopic_detail: Recurrent UTI in infant under 6 months - RCH imaging cascade
stem:
A 4-month-old previously well girl is reviewed in the paediatric clinic with her third confirmed urinary tract infection in 3 months. Each prior episode was suprapubic-aspirate-confirmed E. coli pyelonephritis treated with intravenous cefotaxime for 48 hours then oral co-trimoxazole to complete a 10 day course, with clinical defervescence by 36-48 hours each time.

She is exclusively breastfed, thriving on the 75th centile, with normal milestones and normal nappy output. Antenatal anomaly scan at 20 weeks was reported as normal. Her renal function and electrolytes have been within normal limits throughout. There is no family history of vesicoureteric reflux or renal disease.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132/min, BP 88/52 mmHg, RR 32/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Well, thriving; soft non-tender abdomen; no palpable kidneys or bladder; nappy clear with no offensive odour
data_table.Investigations: Today's mid-stream-equivalent urine culture pending; urine dipstick at clinic positive nitrites and leucocytes; FBC, UEC, CRP within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next imaging investigation?

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram during the current illness to identify and grade any vesicoureteric reflux for grading-based prophylaxis decisions
options.rationale: MCUG is not in the RCH first-line cascade; it is invasive and reserved for selected cases (abnormal ultrasound, family history, very young child with severe recurrent atypical UTI) after ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: DMSA scintigraphy scan in 3 months to assess for cortical scarring as a screening test before considering reflux management options
options.rationale: DMSA is not in the RCH cascade and is a NICE construct; AU practice does not routinely screen for scarring after UTI in well children. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: CT of the kidneys, ureters and bladder under sedation to assess anatomy and exclude stone disease as a recurrent UTI driver in this infant
options.rationale: CT KUB is rarely indicated in paediatric UTI workup due to radiation exposure and low yield in well infants without stone risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound during this current illness to assess anatomy, hydronephrosis, scarring proxies and bladder emptying as the first-line investigation
options.rationale: Per RCH, renal tract ultrasound is the first-line imaging in any infant <6 months with UTI (and in recurrent or atypical UTI at any age); during current illness it picks up acute pyonephrosis, hydronephrosis, and anatomical anomalies. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Repeat urine microscopy and culture monthly for 6 months without any imaging until a fourth episode is confirmed by laboratory testing on collected sample
options.rationale: Surveillance alone misses correctable anatomical drivers; RCH mandates ultrasound after recurrent UTI in any child and after first UTI in infants <6 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: RCH guidance on paediatric UTI imaging is more parsimonious than NICE. Renal tract ultrasound is recommended for: any infant <6 months after first UTI, atypical UTI at any age (poor response to 48h antibiotics, non-E coli organism, sepsis, abdominal mass, raised creatinine), and recurrent UTI at any age. MCUG and DMSA are NOT part of the routine RCH cascade - they are reserved for ultrasound abnormalities or selected high-risk infants. This infant is <6 months AND has recurrent UTI, so ultrasound is doubly indicated.
key_points: RCH first-line imaging in recurrent UTI is renal tract ultrasound.
MCUG and DMSA are not in the RCH routine cascade.
Infants <6 months get ultrasound after first UTI regardless of recurrence.
Ultrasound during current illness picks up acute changes (pyonephrosis).
Prophylactic antibiotics in infants are controversial; not routine without anatomy abnormality.
explanation.pearls: RCH UTI cascade: ultrasound is the universal first step in <6 months or recurrent or atypical UTI; do NOT default to DMSA or MCUG based on NICE-derived habits.

subtopic_detail: Henoch Schonlein purpura with renal involvement
stem:
A 7-year-old boy is reviewed in the paediatric short-stay unit 5 days after developing a non-blanching rash on his buttocks, the back of his thighs and his shins. He has had bilateral ankle pain limiting his walking and complains of cramping tummy pains worse in the evenings. Today his mother noticed his urine has turned a dark cola-coloured shade.

He had a runny nose 10 days ago that has since resolved. On examination there is symmetrical palpable purpura on the buttocks, posterior thighs and shins, with mildly swollen tender ankles bilaterally. Abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness, no peritonism. No oral lesions; no joint deformity. He had a primary varicella vaccination at age 1.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96/min, BP 118/76 mmHg, RR 20/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura on buttocks and lower limbs; symmetrical swollen tender ankles bilaterally; abdomen soft mildly tender, no guarding; no oral ulcers or scleritis
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis blood 3+, protein 2+; urine PCR 180 mg/mmol (<30); serum creatinine 38 micromol/L (estimated GFR 95 mL/min/1.73 m² by Schwartz); FBC, coagulation studies, electrolytes within normal limits; CRP 28 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks now to prevent progression of nephritis and shorten the duration of arthritis pain symptoms
options.rationale: Routine corticosteroids do NOT prevent or reduce HSP nephritis progression per Cochrane and KHA-CARI; they may shorten abdominal pain but are not first-line for renal involvement. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Methylprednisolone pulse 30 mg/kg intravenously daily for 3 days followed by oral taper and renal biopsy this admission to guide further care
options.rationale: Pulse steroids and biopsy are reserved for severe nephritis: nephrotic-range proteinuria, declining renal function, or persistent macroscopic haematuria after 4 weeks; not first-line in moderate proteinuria with normal renal function. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg given as a single infusion over 12 hours for empirical immunomodulation of the cutaneous and renal disease activity
options.rationale: IVIG has no proven role in HSP and is not in any AU first-line guideline; cost and risks are unjustified. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Reassurance with simple analgesia and discharge with GP review in 1 week, and re-presentation guidance for worsening symptoms or new features overall
options.rationale: Visible macroscopic haematuria, proteinuria 2+, and PCR 180 mandate closer monitoring than weekly GP review; missed progression risks end-stage renal disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Admit for blood pressure monitoring, daily urine protein creatinine ratio and serial creatinine over 7 days, with paediatric nephrology referral if proteinuria or hypertension persists
options.rationale: Per RCH, children with HSP and significant renal involvement (PCR >100, persistent macroscopic haematuria, hypertension, declining function) require BP and renal monitoring, with nephrology input if features persist beyond a week. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

explanation.summary: HSP (IgA vasculitis) is the commonest small-vessel vasculitis of childhood and presents with palpable purpura on dependent areas, abdominal pain, arthritis (usually ankles or knees), and renal involvement in 30-50%. The cutaneous and joint disease is self-limiting; renal involvement is the major prognostic determinant. Monitoring strategy: weekly urine dipstick and BP for 6 months minimum; closer surveillance and nephrology referral if PCR rises, macroscopic haematuria persists, or hypertension develops. Steroids do not prevent nephritis but may help abdominal pain.
key_points: HSP: palpable purpura + abdominal pain + arthritis +/- nephritis.
Renal involvement in 30-50%; chief determinant of long-term prognosis.
Monitor urine dipstick and BP weekly for first 6 weeks, then monthly.
Steroids do not prevent nephritis (Cochrane); reserved for severe abdominal pain.
Nephrology referral for nephrotic range proteinuria, declining function, persistent macroscopic haematuria or hypertension.
explanation.pearls: HSP nephritis surveillance is dipstick and BP for at least 6 months - oral prednisolone does NOT prevent progression and is reserved for severe abdominal pain or proven severe nephritis.

subtopic_detail: Kawasaki disease - timing of IVIG within 10 days
stem:
A 3-year-old previously well boy is reviewed in the paediatric emergency department with day 6 of fever to 39.5 °C. He has had a polymorphous truncal rash for 4 days, bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection without exudate, cracked red lips and a strawberry tongue. There is a single right cervical lymph node measuring 2 cm, and his hands and feet became puffy yesterday with desquamation starting around his fingertips today.

He is irritable but consolable, drinking small volumes of fluids. There is no recent travel or sick contacts beyond his older sister with a cold. He completed all primary vaccinations on time. There is no history of immunodeficiency.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138/min, BP 100/60 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 39.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Polymorphous truncal rash; bilateral non-exudative bulbar conjunctivitis; cracked red lips, strawberry tongue; right cervical node 2 cm; non-pitting oedema of hands and feet; peripheral desquamation
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.5 x10⁹/L (5.5-15.5), platelets 580 x10⁹/L (150-400), CRP 180 mg/L (<5), ESR 95 mm/h (<15), ALT 65 U/L (<40), albumin 28 g/L (35-50), urine analysis sterile pyuria 25 leucocytes/HPF

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as a single infusion over 12 hours, plus aspirin 30-50 mg/kg/day in divided doses while febrile and a baseline echocardiogram
options.rationale: He meets clinical criteria for Kawasaki disease (fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 features); IVIG within day 10 of illness reduces coronary aneurysm rate from 25% to 4%; baseline echo guides ongoing monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Aspirin 5 mg/kg daily monotherapy and reassessment for clinical response over the next 48 hours before any other treatment is added to the regimen now
options.rationale: Low-dose aspirin without IVIG fails to reduce coronary aneurysm risk; delay past day 10 substantially increases aneurysm rate and morbidity. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Empirical intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly and ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for presumed staphylococcal or streptococcal scarlet-like illness today
options.rationale: Kawasaki disease clinical features (5+ days fever, mucocutaneous changes, peripheral changes, sterile pyuria) differ from staphylococcal/streptococcal illness; antibiotics do not address the vasculitis or aneurysm risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Defer all definitive therapy until the echocardiogram is performed in the morning to confirm coronary involvement before any IVIG decision is made today
options.rationale: Treatment is not contingent on echo; baseline echo informs follow-up but IVIG must not be delayed when clinical criteria are met within the 10-day window. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Discharge with parental advice on rash care, oral hydration support, paracetamol for fever, and paediatric outpatient follow-up review within the next week
options.rationale: Discharge without IVIG misses the critical treatment window and risks coronary aneurysms; he meets full clinical Kawasaki criteria today. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a medium-vessel vasculitis affecting children under 5, defined by fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 of: bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, mucositis (strawberry tongue, cracked lips), polymorphous rash, peripheral changes (oedema, desquamation), and unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy >=1.5 cm. Without treatment, coronary artery aneurysms develop in approximately 25%; IVIG 2 g/kg given within 10 days of illness onset reduces this to <5%. Aspirin (high-dose anti-inflammatory then low-dose antiplatelet) is added. Echo at baseline and serial intervals (2, 6 weeks) monitors coronary involvement.
key_points: Diagnosis: fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 of CRASH-and-burn features.
IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion within day 10 reduces aneurysms 25% to <5%.
Aspirin: high dose (30-50 mg/kg/day) while febrile, then 3-5 mg/kg/day for 6-8 weeks.
Echo at baseline, 2 weeks, 6 weeks; sooner if features of coronary involvement.
Incomplete/atypical KD in infants is a high-risk subtype - low threshold for IVIG.
explanation.pearls: IVIG within day 10 of Kawasaki cuts coronary aneurysm rate from 25% to under 5% - the diagnostic threshold of 4 out of 5 features alongside fever justifies treating empirically without awaiting echo.

subtopic_detail: Meningococcal disease - immediate empirical management
stem:
A 2-year-old previously well girl is brought to a metropolitan emergency department by her parents after sudden onset fever to 39.5 °C and progressive lethargy over the past 6 hours. Her parents noticed two small purple spots appear on her legs in the last hour that did not fade when pressed with the side of a glass. She has had two episodes of vomiting.

She is drowsy but rousable to voice with weak cry. There is no neck stiffness. On examination peripheries are cool with capillary refill time 4 seconds. Two non-blanching petechiae 6 mm and 4 mm are present on her right shin and left thigh. There is no recent travel; her vaccinations are up to date including meningococcal B and meningococcal ACWY at appropriate ages.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168/min, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 36/min, SpO2 95% room air, temperature 39.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, weak cry; cool peripheries, mottled; CRT 4 seconds; two non-blanching petechiae on lower limbs; no neck stiffness, fontanelle closed
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-6.5); intravenous access yet to be established; rapid bedside whole-blood lactate 4.5 mmol/L (<2.0)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg as a stat dose and arrange urgent transfer by paediatric retrieval to the nearest tertiary paediatric centre overnight
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics in fulminant meningococcaemia are inadequate; IV access (or IO if 2 attempts fail) with immediate IV third-generation cephalosporin is the AU standard before retrieval. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease)

options.text: Establish IV or IO access, give IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg with blood cultures, commence 20 mL/kg sodium chloride 0.9% bolus, and activate paediatric retrieval
options.rationale: Empirical IV ceftriaxone within the first hour, IV/IO access, fluid resuscitation, and retrieval activation is the AU paediatric sepsis pathway; do not delay antibiotics for lumbar puncture in shock. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis assessment and management)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis assessment and management

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture immediately to obtain CSF microscopy and culture before any antibiotic is given so as not to compromise organism identification today
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated in shock or raised ICP; antibiotics must not be delayed for LP. Blood cultures + PCR can identify Neisseria meningitidis post-antibiotic. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease)

options.text: Administer dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg intravenously immediately before any antibiotic is given to reduce inflammatory complications and neurological sequelae from infection
options.rationale: Dexamethasone is given for suspected pneumococcal or Hib meningitis with antibiotics but does not improve outcomes in meningococcal sepsis and should not delay the first antibiotic dose. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease)

options.text: Observe with continuous monitoring for the next 30 minutes while awaiting blood culture results before deciding on definitive antibiotic therapy and access strategy
options.rationale: Mortality in meningococcal sepsis rises with every hour of antibiotic delay; observation is not appropriate when shock signs are present. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal disease)

explanation.summary: Suspected meningococcal disease in a shocked child mandates the AU paediatric sepsis pathway: IV or intraosseous access within 90 seconds (2 failed peripheral attempts triggers IO), blood cultures, first dose of IV ceftriaxone (100 mg/kg) within the hour, 20 mL/kg sodium chloride bolus over 5-10 minutes repeatable to 60 mL/kg, and activation of paediatric retrieval. Lumbar puncture is deferred until shock is corrected and there are no signs of raised ICP. Inotropes (adrenaline) are added after fluids if shock persists. Household contacts require post-exposure ciprofloxacin or rifampicin.
key_points: Time-to-antibiotic in meningococcal sepsis is outcome-determining.
IO access after 2 failed IV attempts or 90 seconds.
Ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV is the AU empirical agent.
Defer LP in shock or raised ICP signs.
Notify Public Health and arrange contact prophylaxis (ciprofloxacin/rifampicin).
explanation.pearls: Ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV/IO within the hour beats every other intervention in meningococcal sepsis - never delay it for LP or further imaging.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Sepsis assessment and management

subtopic_detail: Bacterial tracheitis - airway emergency after failed croup treatment
stem:
A 5-year-old girl who presented to the paediatric emergency department 36 hours ago with classic croup symptoms (barking cough, hoarse voice, mild stridor at rest) was treated with oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and discharged after observation when her stridor settled. She represents today with worsening stridor at rest, high fever to 39 °C, and a toxic appearance. She received a second dose of dexamethasone and one nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 in the ambulance with minimal improvement.

On arrival she sits upright leaning forward and is noticeably distressed but cooperative. There is loud biphasic stridor at rest, marked tracheal tenderness to gentle palpation, and minimal drooling. Auscultation reveals transmitted upper airway sounds. There is no neck mass.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 158/min, BP 105/65 mmHg, RR 42/min, SpO2 91% room air, temperature 39.2 °C
data_table.Examination: Toxic appearance; biphasic stridor at rest; tracheal tenderness on palpation; mild tracheal tug; minimal oral secretions; chest auscultation transmitted sounds only
data_table.Investigations: Blood cultures sent; FBC WCC 22 x10⁹/L (5.5-15.5), CRP 220 mg/L (<5); no imaging yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 and second dose of oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg with ongoing observation in the paediatric resuscitation bay for response
options.rationale: Failure of two doses of adrenaline plus dexamethasone in toxic-appearing high-fever child with tracheal tenderness signals bacterial tracheitis, not refractory croup; ongoing observation risks airway loss. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Obtain a lateral neck X-ray to confirm the diagnosis and exclude epiglottitis before any decision on airway intervention is made in the next hour today
options.rationale: Lateral neck X-ray in a toxic child with stridor is dangerous (can precipitate airway obstruction) and delays definitive airway evaluation; imaging is not first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Urgent ENT and anaesthetic-led airway evaluation in operating theatre under inhalational induction with intubation, then IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly plus IV cefotaxime
options.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis (high fever, toxic appearance, tracheal tenderness, failed croup treatment) is an airway emergency; theatre-based evaluation with intubation and empirical S aureus + H influenzae cover is the AU standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Trial of high-flow heliox 70:30 mixture and high-flow nasal cannula in the paediatric ward bay with reassessment of work of breathing over the next hour while awaiting bloods
options.rationale: Heliox and HFNC do not treat the underlying pseudomembranous bacterial airway disease; definitive airway management and IV antibiotics are required, not non-invasive ventilation alone. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Empirical IV amoxicillin alone targeting Haemophilus influenzae as the most likely organism, with reassessment of airway pending response to antibiotic treatment over the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Amoxicillin alone misses S aureus (most common in bacterial tracheitis) and delays definitive airway management; combined flucloxacillin and cefotaxime is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Bacterial tracheitis presents as croup that does not improve with steroids and nebulised adrenaline, with the child becoming high-fever and toxic-appearing; tracheal tenderness is a classic discriminator. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism. The airway is at high risk of obstruction by pseudomembranous tracheal debris. Management is ENT and anaesthetic-led airway evaluation in theatre (typically intubation under inhalational induction with backup tracheostomy plan), tracheal toilet of debris, and empirical IV antibiotics covering S aureus and H influenzae (flucloxacillin plus cefotaxime). Discriminate from epiglottitis (drooling, tripod, soft stridor without tracheal tenderness).
key_points: Bacterial tracheitis: croup not responding to steroid + adrenaline, plus toxic appearance.
Tracheal tenderness on palpation is a useful discriminator from croup.
Most common organism: Staphylococcus aureus.
Theatre-based airway evaluation by ENT and anaesthetist with intubation.
Antibiotics: flucloxacillin plus cefotaxime IV.
explanation.pearls: When croup fails two doses of adrenaline plus dexamethasone in a toxic febrile child, escalate to theatre for intubation and start flucloxacillin plus cefotaxime IV - bacterial tracheitis kills airways within hours of misdiagnosis as refractory croup.

subtopic_detail: Foreign body aspiration - rigid bronchoscopy under GA
stem:
An 18-month-old boy is brought to the paediatric emergency department after a sudden coughing fit while eating peanuts at his older brother's birthday party 6 hours ago. He recovered quickly with a few coughs but has had a persistent mild cough and intermittent wheeze for the past 4 hours. His parents report he ate a handful of nuts before the choking episode.

He is alert, smiling and not in respiratory distress at rest. On auscultation there are decreased breath sounds and expiratory wheeze localised to the right side. The left side has normal vesicular breath sounds. There is no stridor and no cyanosis. He has no fever and no past respiratory history.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124/min, BP 100/65 mmHg, RR 32/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Comfortable at rest; decreased breath sounds and expiratory wheeze right side; left lung normal vesicular sounds; no stridor, no cyanosis; no chest wall asymmetry
data_table.Investigations: Inspiratory and expiratory chest X-ray shows hyperinflation of right lung on expiratory film with mediastinal shift to left; no radiopaque foreign body visible

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial of nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via face mask, observe respiratory response over the next 60 minutes and discharge if symptoms resolve with parental advice
options.rationale: Foreign body aspiration with classic history and lateralised signs does not respond to bronchodilators; the obstruction is mechanical and requires removal. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration)

options.text: Postural drainage and supervised chest physiotherapy regimen to dislodge the foreign body, with reassessment of breath sounds and air entry afterwards on serial examination
options.rationale: Postural physiotherapy may dislodge a peripheral object into a more proximal airway causing complete obstruction; it is contraindicated as a removal strategy. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration)

options.text: Flexible bronchoscopy under conscious sedation in the radiology suite to visualise and assess airway anatomy before any decision on operative removal is made
options.rationale: Flexible bronchoscopy can be diagnostic but is suboptimal for retrieval of nuts (granulating, friable). Rigid bronchoscopy in theatre under GA is the AU standard for both diagnosis and removal. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration)

options.text: Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia in operating theatre with ENT and anaesthetic teams for both confirmation and definitive removal of the foreign body today
options.rationale: Classic aspiration history, lateralised auscultation signs, and CXR hyperinflation with mediastinal shift mandate rigid bronchoscopy under GA in theatre; peanuts particularly cause chemical bronchitis if retained. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration)

options.text: Discharge home with parental education on red-flag respiratory symptoms and arrange a planned outpatient paediatric review within 48 hours to reassess clinical status
options.rationale: Discharge risks progressive obstruction, post-obstructive pneumonia, and chronic suppurative damage from retained organic foreign body; removal is time-sensitive. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration)

explanation.summary: Foreign body aspiration in toddlers presents with a witnessed choking event followed by lateralised signs (decreased breath sounds, wheeze on one side) and air-trapping on expiratory CXR (hyperinflation, mediastinal shift). Peanuts are the most common organic foreign body in Australia and cause chemical bronchitis if retained. Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia in theatre is the AU standard for both diagnosis and definitive removal. Postural physiotherapy may convert partial to complete obstruction and is contraindicated. Plain CXR may be normal in 30% of cases - history and exam guide the decision to scope, not imaging.
key_points: Toddler + choking history + lateralised signs = aspirated foreign body until proven otherwise.
Expiratory CXR shows hyperinflation on side of obstruction; inspiratory may be normal.
Rigid bronchoscopy under GA in theatre is the AU standard for removal.
Postural physiotherapy is contraindicated; risk of converting partial to complete obstruction.
Peanuts cause chemical bronchitis; remove urgently.
explanation.pearls: A normal CXR does not exclude foreign body aspiration in a toddler with a clear choking history and lateralised signs - rigid bronchoscopy under GA both diagnoses and treats.

subtopic_detail: Testicular torsion - direct to theatre without imaging delay
stem:
A 14-year-old boy is brought to the metropolitan emergency department by his father with a 5 hour history of sudden severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep at 2 am. The pain is constant and rated 9 out of 10. He has had two episodes of vomiting. There is no history of trauma, no recent unprotected sexual activity, and no urinary symptoms.

On examination he is in severe distress lying still. The left hemiscrotum is swollen, exquisitely tender, with the testis riding high and lying transversely. The left cremasteric reflex is absent. The right testis is normal in position and lie with a normal cremasteric reflex. There is no urethral discharge.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 110/min, BP 130/78 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Left hemiscrotum swollen, exquisitely tender; testis high-riding and transverse; absent left cremasteric reflex; right testis normal lie and position with intact cremasteric reflex; no urethral discharge
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis clear; no nitrites, no leucocytes, no blood; FBC and electrolytes within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Urgent colour Doppler ultrasound of the scrotum to assess testicular blood flow before any surgical decision is made on operative management of this presentation
options.rationale: Doppler ultrasound has high false negative rates and delays theatre; clinical diagnosis is sufficient to proceed direct to scrotal exploration in classic presentation within the 6 hour window. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

options.text: Procedural sedation in emergency department with manual detorsion attempt by rotating testis outward (book opening direction) and surgical exploration only if pain does not resolve immediately
options.rationale: Manual detorsion is a temporising measure when surgery is delayed, not a substitute for definitive exploration and bilateral orchidopexy. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

options.text: Empirical IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg and supportive analgesia for presumed acute epididymo-orchitis with reassessment in 4 hours for response to antimicrobial therapy
options.rationale: Epididymo-orchitis is unlikely without urinary symptoms, no urethral discharge, no fever, in a 14 year old with sudden onset pain; missing torsion in the 6 hour window costs the testis. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

options.text: Discharge home with analgesia and urgent next-day outpatient urology clinic review for further assessment and definitive imaging in the morning at the earliest opportunity
options.rationale: Discharge in suspected testicular torsion is negligent; salvage rate drops sharply after 6 hours of ischaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

options.text: Direct transfer to the operating theatre for scrotal exploration, manual detorsion and bilateral orchidopexy under general anaesthesia by the on-call urologist without further investigations
options.rationale: Classic torsion (sudden severe pain, vomiting, high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex) within 5 hours requires immediate theatre; salvage rate approximately 90% under 6 hours, then falls sharply. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

explanation.summary: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency with a 6 hour window for testicular salvage. Classic features (sudden severe pain, vomiting, high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex) should prompt direct theatre without Doppler delay. Bilateral orchidopexy is performed even if the contralateral testis appears normal because the anatomical predisposition (bell-clapper deformity) is bilateral. Differential includes epididymo-orchitis (gradual onset, fever, urinary symptoms, normal cremasteric, pyuria) and torsion of the appendix testis (gradual onset, blue dot sign). Imaging may be reserved for atypical presentations only.
key_points: Salvage rate ~90% under 6 hours; drops to ~50% by 12 hours and near zero by 24 hours.
Direct theatre without Doppler for classic clinical presentation.
Bilateral orchidopexy because bell-clapper is bilateral.
Absent cremasteric reflex has high sensitivity (about 99%).
Manual detorsion (rotate medial to lateral, book opening) only if theatre delay >1 hour.
explanation.pearls: Classic torsion does not need Doppler - the 6 hour clock is more sensitive than imaging. Bilateral orchidopexy is mandatory because the bell-clapper anomaly is bilateral.

sources.label: Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand: Acute scrotum guidance

subtopic_detail: Convulsive status epilepticus - second dose of benzodiazepine
stem:
A 4-year-old previously well boy is brought to the metropolitan emergency department by paramedics after a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began at school 12 minutes ago. Paramedics gave a single dose of buccal midazolam 5 mg en route 6 minutes ago without effect. He continues to seize on arrival.

He has no prior history of seizures. He has been febrile with a 24 hour history of viral upper respiratory tract symptoms. There is no head trauma, no toxic exposure, and no relevant family history. No peripheral intravenous access has yet been established. The on-call paediatric registrar is at bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 162/min, BP 105/68 mmHg, RR 30/min via bag-valve-mask 100% oxygen, SpO2 96% with oxygen, temperature 39.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Continuous generalised tonic-clonic activity; pupils equal and reactive; jaw not relaxed; airway patent with manoeuvres; no purpura, no rash, no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.2 mmol/L (3.5-6.5); ECG sinus tachycardia rate 162; no other investigations yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Intramuscular midazolam 0.15 mg/kg now, while preparing IV or IO access and second-line agent if seizure continues beyond 15 minutes from onset
options.rationale: Second benzodiazepine dose is appropriate at 10 minutes if first dose ineffective and IV access not yet obtained; IM midazolam absorbs reliably; second-line agent (levetiracetam or phenytoin) follows at 15-20 minutes. (Source: APLS Australia Algorithm: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg intravenously over 20 minutes once IV access is obtained as the second-line definitive agent for ongoing convulsive seizure activity in this child
options.rationale: Phenytoin is a valid second-line agent but should follow a second benzodiazepine dose; jumping straight to phenytoin without a second benzo is not the AU APLS pathway. (Source: APLS Australia Algorithm: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Levetiracetam 40 mg/kg intravenously as a single rapid bolus over 5 minutes immediately, without further benzodiazepine, given the seizure has continued past first benzo dose now
options.rationale: Levetiracetam is a valid alternative second-line but should follow a second benzodiazepine; ESETT trial shows levetiracetam, phenytoin, and valproate are all equivalent second-line agents but second benzo precedes them. (Source: APLS Australia Algorithm: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation with thiopentone 5 mg/kg now and admission to paediatric intensive care for thiopentone infusion and continuous EEG monitoring with neurology input
options.rationale: Intubation and thiopentone are reserved for refractory status (>30 minutes despite first and second line); current step is second benzo. (Source: APLS Australia Algorithm: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Urgent CT head before further pharmacological intervention to exclude an acute structural cause for status epilepticus such as bleed or space-occupying lesion or hydrocephalus
options.rationale: Imaging is appropriate after the seizure is controlled and the airway secured; pursuing imaging before stopping the seizure prolongs ictal injury. (Source: APLS Australia Algorithm: Status epilepticus)

explanation.summary: Convulsive status epilepticus follows a time-based algorithm: first benzodiazepine (buccal/IM midazolam, IV lorazepam, or IV diazepam) at 5-10 minutes, second benzodiazepine if still seizing at 10-15 minutes, second-line agent (levetiracetam, phenytoin, or sodium valproate) at 15-25 minutes, and intubation with thiopentone infusion in PICU for refractory status >30 minutes. IM midazolam is a reliable second benzodiazepine when IV access is delayed. ESETT (2019) showed levetiracetam, phenytoin, and valproate are equivalent as second-line agents. Always check glucose, electrolytes, and consider underlying cause once seizure is controlled.
key_points: Time-based algorithm: 1st benzo 5 min, 2nd benzo 10-15 min, 2nd line 15-25 min.
IM midazolam reliable when IV access is delayed.
ESETT: levetiracetam, phenytoin, valproate equivalent for second-line.
Intubation with thiopentone for refractory status >30 minutes.
Check glucose first; treat hypoglycaemia (2 mL/kg 10% dextrose).
explanation.pearls: A second benzodiazepine dose precedes the second-line agent in the APLS Australia algorithm - jumping straight to phenytoin or levetiracetam at 12 minutes is non-standard.

subtopic_detail: Intraosseous access in paediatric resuscitation
stem:
A 2-year-old girl presents to the metropolitan emergency department with septic shock and a 12 hour history of fever, lethargy and reduced wet nappies. She has cool mottled peripheries with delayed capillary refill, is drowsy and minimally responsive. Two attempts at peripheral venous cannulation by the paediatric emergency registrar over the past 75 seconds have failed; both attempts targeted antecubital fossae.

The paediatric retrieval team has been called and is 30 minutes away. The team is preparing for further peripheral attempts. Empirical antibiotics are drawn up and ready. The mother is at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 188/min, BP 65/40 mmHg, RR 50/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 39.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 12; cool mottled peripheries; capillary refill >5 seconds; respiratory rate elevated with shallow effort; no rash or purpura
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 4.5 mmol/L (3.5-6.5); rapid bedside lactate 5.5 mmol/L (<2.0); blood cultures yet to be obtained because peripheral access failed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step for vascular access?

options.text: Continue peripheral intravenous cannulation attempts for up to 5 more minutes by the most experienced operator available before considering alternative access strategies
options.rationale: APLS Australia and RCH paediatric sepsis pathway mandate IO insertion after 2 failed peripheral attempts or 90 seconds in shocked children; further peripheral attempts delay resuscitation. (Source: APLS Australia: Vascular access)

options.text: Insert intraosseous needle into proximal tibia 1-2 cm below tibial tuberosity on medial flat surface, commence resuscitation including fluid bolus 20 mL/kg and IV antibiotics now
options.rationale: AU APLS and RCH sepsis pathway mandate IO insertion after 2 failed peripheral attempts or 90 seconds; proximal tibia is the standard paediatric site; IO can deliver all fluids, drugs and bloods can be drawn. (Source: APLS Australia: Vascular access)

options.text: Wait for paediatric anaesthetist or retrieval team to insert ultrasound-guided central venous catheter once they arrive on site within the next 30 minutes for definitive resuscitation access
options.rationale: Waiting 30 minutes for retrieval risks death from untreated septic shock; IO can be inserted in <60 seconds by any APLS-trained clinician. (Source: APLS Australia: Vascular access)

options.text: Perform venous cut-down to the long saphenous vein at the ankle by paediatric surgical registrar in the next 10 minutes to achieve large-bore access for ongoing resuscitation
options.rationale: Venous cut-down is slower, requires surgical skill, and has largely been replaced by IO insertion in paediatric resuscitation; it is not first-line in 2026. (Source: APLS Australia: Vascular access)

options.text: Administer subcutaneous fluids via the hyaluronidase-assisted hypodermoclysis technique into both thighs while continuing peripheral attempts for next ten minutes with senior clinician
options.rationale: Subcutaneous fluids have no role in shock resuscitation; absorption is too slow. (Source: APLS Australia: Vascular access)

explanation.summary: Intraosseous access is the recommended next step after 2 failed peripheral attempts or 90 seconds in a critically ill child requiring resuscitation. Proximal tibia (1-2 cm below tibial tuberosity on medial flat surface) is the standard paediatric site, with proximal humerus and distal femur as alternatives. IO can deliver all fluids and drugs (including adrenaline, bicarbonate, blood products), and blood can be drawn for cultures and gases. EZ-IO power driver is the AU standard device. IO should be replaced by definitive IV/central access within 24 hours due to risk of osteomyelitis if used longer.
key_points: IO insertion after 2 failed peripheral attempts or 90 seconds in resuscitation.
Proximal tibia: 1-2 cm below tibial tuberosity, medial flat surface.
All fluids and drugs can be given via IO; blood samples can be drawn.
Replace with definitive access within 24 hours.
Avoid in fractured bone or recent IO attempt in same bone.
explanation.pearls: EZ-IO power-driven proximal tibia takes under 60 seconds and accepts every resuscitation drug - waiting for the retrieval team or doing a cut-down in 2026 is an anti-pattern.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Sepsis assessment and management

subtopic_detail: Toxic shock syndrome - clindamycin and source control
stem:
A 14-year-old girl presents to the paediatric emergency department on day 4 of menstruation with fever to 40 °C, diffuse erythematous rash that looks like sunburn, watery diarrhoea and one episode of vomiting over the past 24 hours. She is confused and unable to give a coherent history. She is using high-absorbency tampons.

On examination she is in shock with cool peripheries and capillary refill 4 seconds. The rash is a diffuse blanching erythroderma over the trunk and limbs without target lesions. There is non-purulent conjunctival injection and a strawberry tongue. A tampon is in situ on examination. No surgical wound, no skin abscess. Vaccinations are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 140/min, BP 75/40 mmHg, RR 30/min, SpO2 95% room air, temperature 40.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Confused, GCS 13; diffuse blanching erythroderma; conjunctival injection; strawberry tongue; tampon in situ; cool peripheries CRT 4 seconds; no abscess or wound
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18 x10⁹/L (4-11), platelets 130 x10⁹/L (150-400), Na 130 mmol/L (135-145), creatinine 180 micromol/L (45-90), CK 880 U/L (<200), ALT 95 U/L (<40), INR 1.4 (0.9-1.2), lactate 4.5 mmol/L (<2.0); blood cultures sent

lead_in: After IV access, 20 mL/kg sodium chloride bolus, and PICU activation, what is the most appropriate antimicrobial and source control strategy?

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 15 mg/kg six-hourly monotherapy with tampon removal at presentation and supportive intensive care management of fluid resuscitation and inotropes
options.rationale: Vancomycin alone does not suppress toxin production; clindamycin is added specifically to inhibit ribosomal toxin synthesis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg daily and metronidazole 12.5 mg/kg eight-hourly without tampon removal, escalation only if antibiotic response is poor over the first 24 hours
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone + metronidazole covers intra-abdominal sepsis, not staphylococcal TSS; source control by tampon removal is the first priority in menstrual TSS. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly plus clindamycin 15 mg/kg eight-hourly, immediate tampon removal as source control, and consider IVIG 1-2 g/kg if shock is refractory
options.rationale: Menstrual TSS is staphylococcal toxin-mediated; flucloxacillin treats organism, clindamycin suppresses toxin synthesis at the ribosome, tampon removal is source control, IVIG neutralises circulating toxin in severe cases. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome

options.text: Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 100 mg/kg six-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy with delayed tampon removal once shock has been adequately stabilised over the first 4 hours of care
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam is broad-spectrum but inferior anti-staphylococcal activity to flucloxacillin and does not suppress toxin; delayed source control increases ongoing toxin release. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome

options.text: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily plus intravenous gentamicin 7 mg/kg daily for empirical sepsis cover pending blood culture and microbiology susceptibility results overnight
options.rationale: Oral therapy in shocked patient is inappropriate; gentamicin lacks anti-staphylococcal toxin suppression activity; doxycycline is not adequate monotherapy in fulminant TSS. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome

explanation.summary: Staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome is a toxin-mediated illness presenting with fever, hypotension, diffuse erythroderma, multi-organ dysfunction and (later) desquamation. Menstrual TSS is associated with prolonged tampon use; non-menstrual TSS with surgical/burn wounds or skin abscesses. Management has three pillars: (1) organism eradication with flucloxacillin (or vancomycin if MRSA suspected), (2) toxin suppression with clindamycin (binds ribosome inhibiting exotoxin synthesis), and (3) source control (tampon removal, wound debridement). IVIG 1-2 g/kg is considered for refractory shock to neutralise circulating superantigen. Streptococcal TSS is the differential and is treated similarly (penicillin + clindamycin).
key_points: Three pillars: anti-staphylococcal antibiotic + clindamycin (toxin suppression) + source control.
Clindamycin binds ribosome to inhibit exotoxin synthesis - critical adjunct.
Tampon removal is immediate source control in menstrual TSS.
IVIG 1-2 g/kg for refractory shock to neutralise superantigen.
Differential streptococcal TSS treated with penicillin + clindamycin.
explanation.pearls: Clindamycin within the first hour suppresses ongoing toxin production at the ribosome - flucloxacillin alone kills the organism but does not switch off the exotoxin already being made.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Toxic shock syndrome
RCH CPG: Sepsis assessment and management

subtopic_detail: Exertional heatstroke - cold water immersion target
stem:
A 16-year-old male competitive runner collapses 9 km into a 10 km charity run on a 38 °C ambient temperature day. Paramedics arrive within 8 minutes and find him confused, ataxic, with hot dry red skin and a tympanic core temperature of 41.2 °C. There is no history of seizure, drug use, or psychiatric medication. He has no significant medical history.

On arrival to the paediatric emergency department he is confused (GCS 13), unable to stand, with profound hyperthermia. He is hypotensive and tachycardic. He has not had any oral fluids since the start of the race 90 minutes ago and last urinated 4 hours ago before the start.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 140/min, BP 95/55 mmHg, RR 32/min, SpO2 99% room air, tympanic temperature 41.2 °C
data_table.Examination: Confused GCS 13; hot dry red skin; no rash, no neck stiffness; ataxic gait; muscles tender to palpation; no focal neurological deficit
data_table.Investigations: Na 148 mmol/L (135-145), K 5.5 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 200 micromol/L (45-90), CK 8000 U/L (<200), lactate 6.2 mmol/L (<2.0), pH 7.30, HCO3 16 mmol/L (22-26); urinalysis 3+ blood, no red cells on microscopy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate cooling strategy?

options.text: Tepid sponging and oral cold fluids in resus bay with active cooling fan only, antipyretic paracetamol 1 g orally, monitor over the next hour for response to passive cooling measures
options.rationale: Tepid sponging is too slow for exertional heatstroke with core >40 °C; paracetamol does not lower temperature in non-pyrogen-mediated hyperthermia. (Source: Sports Medicine Australia: Heat illness)

options.text: Paracetamol 1 g orally and evaporative cooling with continuous fanning of misted skin alone in resus bay aiming to cool gradually over the next 60 minutes with reassessment
options.rationale: Evaporative cooling is acceptable when CWI is unavailable but is slower (cooling rate 0.05 vs 0.2 °C/min) and inferior to CWI for exertional heatstroke; paracetamol is ineffective. (Source: Sports Medicine Australia: Heat illness)

options.text: Endovascular cooling catheter insertion via right femoral vein in resus bay with target temperature 36 °C over the next 60 minutes under continuous arterial line monitoring of core
options.rationale: Endovascular cooling has slower cooling rate than CWI, requires expertise and equipment usually unavailable in ED, and is reserved for ICU when other methods fail. (Source: Sports Medicine Australia: Heat illness)

options.text: Immediate cold water immersion target core temperature of 39 °C achieved within 30 minutes, with continuous rectal probe core temperature monitoring throughout the cooling intervention period
options.rationale: Cold water immersion (2-15 °C) targeting cool-to-39 °C within 30 minutes is the most rapid cooling method (rate 0.2 °C/min) and has the lowest mortality in exertional heatstroke; stopping at 39 °C avoids overshoot to hypothermia. (Source: Sports Medicine Australia: Heat illness)

options.text: Whole body application of ice packs to neck axillae and groin without water immersion, with continuous rectal probe monitoring of core temperature to a target of 36 °C over 60 minutes
options.rationale: Ice packs at major vessels are slower than CWI (cooling rate 0.03 °C/min) and inferior for exertional heatstroke; targeting 36 °C risks overshoot to hypothermia. (Source: Sports Medicine Australia: Heat illness)

explanation.summary: Exertional heatstroke in young athletes is defined by core temperature >40 °C with central nervous system dysfunction in the context of exertion. Cold water immersion (CWI) at 2-15 °C is the fastest cooling method (rate 0.15-0.2 °C/min) and is associated with the lowest mortality when applied within 30 minutes of collapse. The target is to cool TO 39 °C within 30 minutes - not lower - to avoid overshoot hypothermia. Evaporative cooling is an acceptable alternative when CWI is not feasible. Complications include rhabdomyolysis (CK >5000), AKI, DIC, hepatic injury, and arrhythmias - resuscitation with IV crystalloid and electrolyte correction proceeds concurrently. Paracetamol and dantrolene do NOT have a role.
key_points: Cold water immersion is first-line for exertional heatstroke; cooling rate ~0.2 °C/min.
Target cool to 39 °C within 30 minutes; avoid overshoot to hypothermia.
Evaporative cooling is acceptable when CWI unavailable but slower.
Paracetamol has no role in exertional hyperthermia (no pyrogen).
Monitor for rhabdomyolysis (CK >5000), AKI, DIC, hepatic injury.
explanation.pearls: Cold water immersion cools at 0.2 °C per minute - target 39 °C then stop, because overshoot hypothermia kills as readily as the original hyperthermia.

subtopic_detail: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula recognition at first feed
stem:
A newborn boy at 12 hours of life, born at 39 weeks via uncomplicated normal vaginal birth to a primigravida mother, develops frothy white secretions from the mouth and brief desaturation to 85% during his first attempted breastfeed in the postnatal ward. He coughed, spluttered, and recovered to baseline saturations on room air within 90 seconds without intervention. He has been spitting up small amounts of clear fluid between feeds since birth.

Antenatal anomaly scan at 20 weeks was reported as polyhydramnios with amniotic fluid index 26 cm; no other anomaly was visualised. Family history is non-contributory. On examination he is alert and pink on room air. The abdomen is scaphoid. There is no facial or limb anomaly. Cardiac examination is normal with no murmur.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 148/min, BP 70/40 mmHg, RR 50/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, pink on air; frothy oral secretions reaccumulating; scaphoid abdomen; no facial dysmorphism; symmetric chest movements; no cardiac murmur; anus patent
data_table.Investigations: Routine antenatal serology negative; no postnatal investigations yet performed; no vertebral or limb anomaly on cursory examination

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial of paced bottle feeds with thickened formula and reassessment of feeding tolerance over the next 24 hours in the postnatal ward with lactation consultant support and review
options.rationale: Thickened formula in tracheo-oesophageal fistula risks ongoing aspiration; the clinical picture (frothy oral secretions, choking with feed, scaphoid abdomen with polyhydramnios) demands urgent workup. (Source: RCH CPG: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula)

options.text: Discharge with home apnoea monitor and lactation support with paediatric outpatient review within 7 days for ongoing reassessment of feeding and breathing in the home environment
options.rationale: Suspected TOF cannot be discharged; failure to recognise leads to repeated aspiration, pneumonia, and abdominal distension from gas via fistula. (Source: RCH CPG: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula)

options.text: Echocardiogram only as the priority investigation today, as cardiac anomalies are common in this cohort, before any consideration of further alimentary tract investigations or surgical referral
options.rationale: Echo is part of VACTERL workup but does not diagnose TOF; passage of orogastric tube with chest and abdomen X-ray is the immediate confirmation. (Source: RCH CPG: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula)

options.text: Barium swallow study under fluoroscopy this morning to dynamically assess swallowing mechanics and detect any tracheal communication before paediatric surgical referral is finalised today
options.rationale: Contrast swallow is CONTRAINDICATED in suspected TOF due to aspiration into lungs causing chemical pneumonitis; OGT + plain film is the safe diagnostic approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula)

options.text: Pass a 10 French orogastric tube to estimate distance to coiling or resistance, then obtain a plain chest and abdominal X-ray to assess tube position and gas pattern, and refer to paediatric surgery
options.rationale: OGT passage with coiling at 10-12 cm plus gas-filled stomach on plain X-ray confirms oesophageal atresia with distal TOF (most common type); contrast study is contraindicated due to aspiration risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula)

explanation.summary: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula presents in the neonate with the classic triad of frothy oral secretions, choking/coughing/cyanosis with first feeds, and inability to pass an orogastric tube. Antenatal polyhydramnios (from inability to swallow amniotic fluid) is suggestive. The most common type (85%) is oesophageal atresia with distal TOF (Gross type C) - identified by OGT coiling in upper pouch at 10-12 cm with gas-filled stomach on plain abdominal film (gas reaches stomach via the distal fistula). Pure oesophageal atresia (no fistula) shows OGT coiling AND a gasless abdomen. Contrast studies are contraindicated due to aspiration into the lungs causing chemical pneumonitis. Look for VACTERL associations (Vertebral, Anorectal, Cardiac, TE, Renal, Limb). Definitive management is paediatric surgical repair.
key_points: Triad: frothy oral secretions, choking with first feeds, OGT cannot pass.
Antenatal polyhydramnios is suggestive.
OGT coiling at 10-12 cm + gas in stomach on CXR = Gross type C (85%).
Contrast swallow CONTRAINDICATED (aspiration risk).
Screen for VACTERL associations: vertebral, anorectal, cardiac, renal, limb.
explanation.pearls: OGT plus a plain chest-and-abdomen X-ray confirms TOF in under 10 minutes - never order a barium swallow in a neonate with frothy oral secretions, the contrast will spill into the lungs.

subtopic_detail: Placental abruption with foetal compromise at 34 weeks
stem:
A 32-year-old G3P2 woman at 34+5 weeks gestation presents to the obstetric assessment unit with sudden onset severe constant abdominal pain and dark vaginal bleeding starting 60 minutes ago. Her two previous deliveries were emergency caesarean sections. She has chronic hypertension managed on labetalol 200 mg twice daily; antenatal care has otherwise been uncomplicated and the foetus has been growing on the 50th centile.

On examination the uterus is tense, painful, and woody-hard to palpation. The cardiotocograph shows a sustained foetal bradycardia at 90 bpm with reduced variability. Vaginal examination reveals approximately 300 mL of dark blood, cervix posterior, 1 cm dilated, no cord felt. The patient is alert and oriented but anxious.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal pulse 110/min, BP 145/95 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C; foetal HR 90/min sustained
data_table.Examination: Tense painful woody uterus; CTG sustained foetal bradycardia 90 bpm reduced variability; vaginal exam 300 mL dark blood, cervix 1 cm posterior, no cord palpable
data_table.Investigations: Group and save sent; FBC haemoglobin 105 g/L (115-165), platelets 195 x10⁹/L (150-400); coagulation studies pending; bedside ultrasound shows retroplacental clot

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Activate massive transfusion protocol, two large bore IV cannulae, IV crystalloid resuscitation, and category 1 caesarean section within 30 minutes by the on call obstetric team
options.rationale: Sustained foetal bradycardia with abruption and maternal compromise mandates category 1 caesarean (decision-to-delivery <30 min); MTP activation pre-empts coagulopathy from abruption-induced DIC. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Perform immediate amniotomy to expedite assisted vaginal delivery with low cavity forceps once cervix is fully dilated under regional anaesthesia, with continuous CTG monitoring during birth
options.rationale: Cervix is 1 cm dilated and not amenable to expedited vaginal birth; abruption with bradycardia and 2 prior CS requires category 1 caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Commence tocolysis with nifedipine 20 mg orally and antenatal betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscular before delivery to optimise foetal lung maturity before scheduling caesarean within 24 hours
options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in active significant abruption with foetal compromise; the foetus requires immediate delivery, not lung maturation that will not benefit in 12-24 hours. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Conservative management with strict bedrest, continuous CTG monitoring on the antenatal ward, and reassessment for delivery decision after 24 hours of observation and serial bloods
options.rationale: Conservative management is reserved for stable abruption without foetal compromise or coagulopathy; sustained bradycardia mandates immediate delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Attempt external cephalic version to confirm cephalic presentation followed by induction of labour with oxytocin and amniotomy under continuous CTG monitoring during the next 6 hours
options.rationale: ECV in active abruption with bradycardia would worsen abruption; induction takes time the foetus does not have; immediate caesarean is required. (Source: RANZCOG Antepartum haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Placental abruption with foetal compromise (CTG bradycardia or repetitive late decelerations) and maternal haemodynamic concern is a category 1 obstetric emergency requiring delivery within 30 minutes. Risk factors include hypertension (chronic or pre-eclampsia), trauma, smoking, cocaine use, and prior abruption. Major haemorrhage is associated with DIC due to placental tissue factor release - MTP activation should be considered early. Maternal blood loss is often underestimated as concealed retroplacental haemorrhage. Disseminated intravascular coagulation, acute kidney injury, and postpartum haemorrhage are major sequelae.
key_points: Cat 1 caesarean: decision-to-delivery <30 minutes.
MTP activation early to pre-empt DIC.
Concealed haemorrhage often underestimated.
Tocolysis contraindicated in active abruption with compromise.
DIC, AKI, and PPH are major sequelae.
explanation.pearls: Tense painful woody uterus plus sustained foetal bradycardia is a 30 minute clock - activate MTP at the same time as you call category 1, because abruption DIC starts before you cut.

subtopic_detail: PCOS ovulation induction first-line letrozole
stem:
A 29-year-old woman is seen in the fertility clinic with 18 months of primary infertility. She has oligomenorrhoea since menarche (4-6 cycles per year), hirsutism (modified Ferriman-Gallwey 12), and acne. Polycystic ovarian syndrome was diagnosed 4 years ago by Rotterdam criteria. Her body mass index is 33 kg/m². Over the past 6 months she has lost 8% of her body weight with dietitian and exercise physiologist input and has had 2 spontaneous cycles in that time.

Her male partner has a normal semen analysis. Hysterosalpingogram demonstrates patent fallopian tubes bilaterally. Her AMH is 78 pmol/L (3-30) and antral follicle count is 35 (12-25). Day 21 progesterone in her last cycle was 5 nmol/L (>30 confirms ovulation). She has no diabetes; HbA1c 5.1%.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 128/82 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: BMI 33; modified Ferriman-Gallwey 12; mild facial acne; no acanthosis nigricans; pelvic examination unremarkable; transvaginal ultrasound bilateral polycystic ovary morphology
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8 mIU/L (0.4-4.0); prolactin 280 mIU/L (<500 non-pregnant); HbA1c 5.1%; AMH 78 pmol/L (3-30); 21-day progesterone 5 nmol/L (>30 ovulation confirmation)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line ovulation induction agent?

options.text: Clomiphene citrate 50 mg orally days 2-6 of cycle for up to six ovulatory cycles with cycle monitoring by transvaginal ultrasound and day 21 progesterone
options.rationale: Clomiphene was previously first-line but PCOS Australia/RANZCOG and the 2023 International PCOS Guideline now recommend letrozole as superior first-line (higher live birth rate, lower twin rate). (Source: RANZCOG PCOS guidance)

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg orally on days 2-6 of cycle for up to six ovulatory cycles with cycle monitoring by transvaginal ultrasound and day 21 progesterone for ovulation confirmation
options.rationale: Letrozole is now first-line for PCOS ovulation induction per the 2023 International PCOS Guideline endorsed by RANZCOG; higher live birth rate vs clomiphene (PPCOS II trial NEJM 2014), with lower multiple pregnancy rate. (Source: RANZCOG PCOS guidance)

options.text: Recombinant follicle stimulating hormone injection from day 2 of the cycle with cycle monitoring by transvaginal ultrasound and hCG trigger when lead follicle reaches 18 mm in diameter
options.rationale: Gonadotrophins are second-line after oral agents fail; significantly higher risk of multiple pregnancy and OHSS, requiring more intensive monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG PCOS guidance)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill for three months to suppress and reset the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, then a fourth cycle with hCG trigger after spontaneous follicle development
options.rationale: COCP suppresses ovulation rather than inducing it; this approach delays fertility treatment by 3 months without benefit. (Source: RANZCOG PCOS guidance)

options.text: Refer for in vitro fertilisation with gonadotrophin releasing hormone antagonist protocol and elective single embryo transfer before any first-line ovulation induction trial in this couple
options.rationale: IVF is reserved for failure of oral and gonadotrophin ovulation induction or for additional infertility factors; first-line is letrozole. (Source: RANZCOG PCOS guidance)

explanation.summary: The 2023 International Evidence-based Guideline for the Assessment and Management of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (endorsed by RANZCOG and Monash University) now recommends letrozole as the first-line pharmacological agent for ovulation induction in anovulatory PCOS, based on PPCOS II (Legro et al, NEJM 2014) showing higher live birth rate (27.5% vs 19.1%) and lower multiple pregnancy rate compared with clomiphene. Weight loss of 5-10% remains the first-line non-pharmacological intervention. Metformin can be added as adjunct, particularly in BMI >30. Gonadotrophins or IVF are reserved for failure of oral therapy.
key_points: Letrozole now first-line for PCOS ovulation induction (RANZCOG 2023).
Higher live birth rate and lower multiples vs clomiphene (PPCOS II).
Letrozole 2.5 mg days 2-6, escalate to 5 mg then 7.5 mg if needed.
Weight loss of 5-10% improves spontaneous ovulation and treatment success.
Gonadotrophins and IVF reserved for oral therapy failure.
explanation.pearls: Letrozole 2.5 mg days 2-6 has displaced clomiphene as first-line for PCOS ovulation induction in Australia - higher live birth, lower twin rate, and unbothered by clomiphene's antiestrogenic endometrial effects.

subtopic_detail: Acute uterine inversion - immediate replacement with placenta in situ
stem:
A 28-year-old G1P1 has just had a normal vaginal birth of a 4.0 kg infant after a prolonged second stage. Active management of the third stage was performed with intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU. Controlled cord traction was applied while the registrar assessed uterine tone, which was felt to be soft. Within seconds of traction the patient develops sudden severe abdominopelvic pain and looks pale.

On rapid reassessment her pulse is 38 beats per minute and blood pressure 65/30 mmHg. She remains conscious but distressed.
