Inspection of the perineum reveals an inverted boggy mass protruding through the introitus with the placenta still attached and approximately 600 mL of blood loss already documented. The anaesthetist is in the room.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 38/min, BP 65/30 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 36.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Pale and distressed but conscious; abdominal palpation: uterine fundus not palpable; inverted boggy mass at introitus with attached placenta; estimated blood loss 600 mL ongoing
data_table.Investigations: FBC pre-delivery haemoglobin 122 g/L (115-165); group and save active; coagulation studies sent; no other investigations completed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Administer ergometrine 250 mcg intravenously immediately to contract the uterus before attempting replacement of the inverted fundus through the cervical ring during continuing resuscitation
options.rationale: Uterotonics BEFORE replacement worsen the cervical ring contracting around the inverted fundus, making replacement impossible; uterotonics are given only AFTER successful replacement. (Source: RANZCOG Major obstetric haemorrhage)

options.text: Remove the placenta first by manual separation before attempting to replace the inverted uterus through the cervical ring as ongoing resuscitation continues with IV access and fluids
options.rationale: Removing placenta from an inverted uterus dramatically worsens haemorrhage; leave placenta in situ during replacement and remove only after the uterus is replaced and contracted. (Source: RANZCOG Major obstetric haemorrhage)

options.text: Immediate manual replacement of the uterine fundus via the Johnson manoeuvre with the placenta still attached, while resuscitating with IV fluids and giving atropine 600 mcg IV for vagal bradycardia
options.rationale: Immediate replacement (Johnson manoeuvre) with placenta still attached is the AU first-line; atropine treats the parasympathetic-mediated bradycardia from cervical ring stretch; uterotonics follow once replaced. (Source: RANZCOG Major obstetric haemorrhage)

options.text: Urgent transfer to operating theatre for surgical replacement under general anaesthesia using the Huntington or Haultain procedure with no immediate attempt at manual replacement now
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed manual replacement; the longer the delay, the more the cervical ring contracts and the harder replacement becomes. Manual attempt first. (Source: RANZCOG Major obstetric haemorrhage)

options.text: Bimanual uterine compression with intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes and packing of the inverted segment without any attempt at replacement during ongoing fluid resuscitation today
options.rationale: Compression and packing does not address the inversion; only replacement restores anatomy and stops bleeding. TXA is appropriate adjunct AFTER replacement. (Source: RANZCOG Major obstetric haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Acute uterine inversion is a rare obstetric emergency (1 in 2000-20000 births) characterised by sudden maternal collapse out of proportion to blood loss (vagal-mediated bradycardia and hypotension from cervical ring stretch) combined with PPH. Diagnosis is made on inspection (inverted fundus visible at introitus) or palpation (non-palpable fundus abdominally). Management: (1) immediate manual replacement (Johnson manoeuvre - upward pressure on inverted fundus with palm of hand) with placenta still attached, (2) resuscitation with IV fluids and atropine for vagal bradycardia, (3) uterotonics ONLY after replacement (oxytocin then ergometrine if no hypertension), (4) surgical replacement if manual fails (Huntington abdominal traction, Haultain posterior incision of cervical ring). Key teaching points: do NOT remove placenta before replacement, do NOT give uterotonics before replacement.
key_points: Sudden collapse + bradycardia + non-palpable fundus + inverted mass at introitus.
Johnson manoeuvre: immediate manual replacement with placenta still attached.
Atropine 600 mcg IV for vagal bradycardia.
Uterotonics ONLY after replacement (otherwise cervical ring traps fundus).
Surgical (Huntington or Haultain) if manual replacement fails.
explanation.pearls: In acute uterine inversion, leaving the placenta attached and skipping uterotonics until after Johnson manoeuvre replacement is the counter-intuitive sequence that saves the uterus and the patient.

subtopic_detail: Amniotic fluid embolism with coagulopathy
stem:
A 38-year-old G4P3 at 40+2 weeks gestation had labour induced for postdates pregnancy. She has just had a normal vaginal birth of a 3.6 kg infant. Within 90 seconds of placental delivery she becomes acutely dyspnoeic, agitated and complains of an impending sense of doom, then rapidly loses consciousness. The midwife notes ongoing brisk vaginal bleeding that does not clot when applied to gauze.

The anaesthetist intubates the patient. Brief cardiac compressions are commenced before pulses return with low-dose adrenaline. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with a right heart strain pattern (S1Q3T3). Bedside echocardiogram performed by the intensivist shows a dilated right ventricle with septal flattening. There is no rash, no recent antibiotic exposure.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 140/min after adrenaline, BP 70/40 mmHg, RR ventilated, SpO2 88% on FiO2 1.0, temperature 36.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Intubated and ventilated; right heart strain on ECG and bedside echo with RV dilation and septal flattening; profuse non-clotting vaginal blood; estimated blood loss 1500 mL and rising
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 88 g/L (115-165), platelets 70 x10⁹/L (150-400), INR 2.4 (0.9-1.2), fibrinogen 0.8 g/L (2.0-4.5); group O Rh positive; cross-match in progress; troponin pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate blood-product strategy now?

options.text: Crystalloid resuscitation only with permissive hypotension targeting MAP 50 mmHg until cross matched packed red cells become available within the next 45 minutes from blood bank
options.rationale: Crystalloid alone in AFE-associated DIC worsens dilutional coagulopathy; immediate balanced blood product activation is required, not permissive hypotension in a pregnant collapsed patient. (Source: RANZCOG Major obstetric haemorrhage)

options.text: Packed red blood cells alone targeting haemoglobin above 70 g/L without immediate fresh frozen plasma or cryoprecipitate until further coagulation studies are available for review by haematology
options.rationale: Red cells alone in DIC dilutes already deficient coagulation factors and fibrinogen; balanced 1:1:1 MTP is the AU standard from the outset. (Source: NBA: Patient Blood Management - Obstetric)

options.text: Packed red cells with one early unit of cryoprecipitate but defer fresh frozen plasma transfusion until repeat fibrinogen returns from the laboratory in approximately 30 minutes
options.rationale: Delaying FFP and platelets in AFE-DIC worsens coagulopathy and PPH; AU MTP gives RBC, FFP, platelets and cryoprecipitate in balanced ratios immediately. (Source: NBA: Patient Blood Management - Obstetric)

options.text: Activate massive transfusion protocol with empirical 1:1:1 packed red cells, fresh frozen plasma and platelets, cryoprecipitate to keep fibrinogen above 2 g/L, plus tranexamic acid 1 g IV
options.rationale: AU MTP for obstetric haemorrhage gives balanced 1:1:1 ratio with cryoprecipitate (obstetric fibrinogen target >2 g/L, higher than non-obstetric); TXA <3 h from haemorrhage onset reduces mortality (WOMAN trial). (Source: NBA: Patient Blood Management - Obstetric)

options.text: Four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate 25 units per kilogram intravenously as the sole haemostatic resuscitation strategy without balanced blood products until further investigations available
options.rationale: PCC is not first-line in AFE-DIC; it lacks fibrinogen and platelets, both critical in this coagulopathy. Balanced MTP is the standard. (Source: NBA: Patient Blood Management - Obstetric)

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare (1 in 20,000-80,000 births) but devastating obstetric emergency presenting with sudden cardiovascular collapse, respiratory failure, and DIC during or shortly after labour and birth. Pathophysiology involves anaphylactoid response to foetal antigens entering maternal circulation, causing acute pulmonary vasoconstriction (RV failure) followed by left heart failure and consumptive coagulopathy. Diagnosis is clinical. Management is supportive: (1) high quality CPR if arrest, with left lateral tilt; (2) intubation and ventilation; (3) circulatory support with inotropes; (4) balanced massive transfusion targeting fibrinogen >2 g/L; (5) TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours; (6) perimortem caesarean within 4-5 minutes if arrest and undelivered. Maternal mortality remains 20-60%.
key_points: AFE: sudden collapse + respiratory failure + DIC in/after labour.
Pathophysiology: anaphylactoid response, RV failure, then coagulopathy.
Balanced 1:1:1 MTP from outset, target fibrinogen >2 g/L.
TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours of haemorrhage (WOMAN trial).
Perimortem caesarean within 4-5 min if arrest and undelivered foetus.
explanation.pearls: Obstetric fibrinogen target is over 2 g/L (higher than non-obstetric 1.5) because pregnancy doubles the normal range and DIC consumes it fast - cryoprecipitate goes in early during MTP for AFE.

subtopic_detail: Valproate in women of childbearing potential - switch planning
stem:
A 22-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is reviewed in the psychiatry outpatient clinic. She has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for the past 3 years with full remission. Her past episodes were all manic with psychotic features, with no significant depressive episodes recorded. She has recently become engaged and discloses that she would like to conceive within the next 1-2 years.

She has read about valproate-associated congenital malformations and neurodevelopmental effects and asks how to plan ahead safely. She has never had a trial of lithium or atypical antipsychotic monotherapy. She drinks no alcohol, does not smoke, and her thyroid function and renal function are within normal limits. There is no family history of bipolar disorder treatment failure.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 122/78 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Euthymic; well groomed; no psychomotor disturbance; insight intact; future-oriented goals appropriate to current life stage
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 410 micromol/L (350-700); TSH 1.6 mIU/L (0.4-4.0); creatinine 65 micromol/L (45-90), eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73 m²; BHCG negative this morning at clinic

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in medication planning?

options.text: Continue current dose of valproate with high dose folate 5 mg daily, detailed pre-conception counselling about teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risks, and proceed to conception when ready
options.rationale: Valproate has the highest teratogenic risk (10% major malformation, 30-40% neurodevelopmental impact); folate does not abolish that risk and continuation in WOCBP is contraindicated where alternatives exist. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Cease valproate abruptly now and observe medication free for the next 6 months to confirm stability before any other agent is introduced or pregnancy is planned by the patient
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of valproate in bipolar I with manic-psychotic episodes carries high relapse risk; switch under cover to an alternative agent before stopping, do not leave untreated. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Switch directly to carbamazepine 200 mg twice daily as the primary alternative mood stabiliser with the same efficacy profile and a more favourable pregnancy safety profile than valproate
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is also a category D teratogen (neural tube defects, craniofacial defects) and is no safer than valproate in pregnancy; not appropriate first choice. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Switch to olanzapine 10 mg orally nocte as the maintenance agent for mania-predominant bipolar I with planned pregnancy in the next 2 years, with monthly mental state and metabolic monitoring
options.rationale: Olanzapine is acceptable in pregnancy but lithium is the AU first-line for mania-predominant bipolar with planned pregnancy due to superior anti-manic prophylaxis and well characterised pregnancy data. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Switch under specialist supervision to lithium with serum level and mood monitoring, confirm stability for at least 6 months on the new agent before conception, and arrange foetal echocardiography in pregnancy
options.rationale: Lithium is RANZCP first-line for mania-predominant bipolar in WOCBP planning pregnancy; absolute Ebstein anomaly risk is approximately 1 in 1000; switch under cover, confirm stability 6 months before conception, and arrange foetal echocardiography at 18-20 weeks. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate has the worst teratogenic and neurodevelopmental profile of all antiepileptics and mood stabilisers (major malformation rate 10%; 30-40% neurodevelopmental impact). The TGA has issued a black-box restriction on valproate in women of childbearing potential without a Pregnancy Prevention Programme. For bipolar with predominantly manic episodes, lithium is the AU first-line alternative for women planning pregnancy because of superior anti-manic prophylaxis; the absolute Ebstein anomaly risk is around 1 in 1000 (recent data revise the originally cited 1 in 100). Switch is performed under specialist supervision with at least 6 months of stability on lithium before conception, foetal echocardiography at 18-20 weeks, third-trimester lithium dose review, and discussion of breastfeeding (lithium is relatively contraindicated). Lamotrigine is preferred for depression-predominant bipolar.
key_points: Valproate is contraindicated in WOCBP without a Pregnancy Prevention Programme (TGA).
Lithium first-line for mania-predominant bipolar planning pregnancy.
Absolute Ebstein anomaly risk on lithium ~1 in 1000.
Confirm 6 months of stability before conception attempts.
Lamotrigine first-line if depression-predominant bipolar.
explanation.pearls: Ebstein anomaly absolute risk on lithium is about 1 in 1000 - the historical 1 in 100 figure overstated risk and modern RANZCP guidance favours lithium over valproate for mania-predominant bipolar in WOCBP.

sources.label: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Bipolar in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Pre-stimulant cardiovascular screen
stem:
A 28-year-old male software developer is reviewed in the general adult psychiatry outpatient clinic with 6 months of persistent inattention, distractibility, restlessness and difficulty completing tasks at work. School reports document similar features from age 7. ASRS-v1.1 screener is 6 of 6 positive. He requests stimulant treatment after researching options online.

His mother had a myocardial infarction at age 55. His father has hypertension on amlodipine. He has no personal history of cardiac symptoms, syncope, palpitations or exercise intolerance. He drinks 4 standard drinks per week and uses no illicit drugs. His weight is 75 kg and BMI 24. There are no features of psychosis or current mood disorder. He has not previously trialled stimulants or atomoxetine.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 120/78 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mental state appropriate to interview; affect reactive; no psychomotor disturbance; cardiac auscultation dual heart sounds no murmurs; no signs of anxiety disorder beyond ADHD-attributable restlessness
data_table.Investigations: FBC, electrolytes, thyroid function, liver function within normal limits; urine drug screen negative; no ECG or cardiac investigations performed to date

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step before initiating treatment?

options.text: Perform 12-lead ECG and obtain a focused personal and family cardiovascular history with examination before commencing stimulant therapy and arrange a 3 month BP and HR review on treatment
options.rationale: RACGP, AADPA and RANZCP recommend baseline 12-lead ECG plus focused CV history and exam (palpitations, syncope, exercise intolerance, family history of sudden death <40 years) before initiating stimulants; routine cardiology referral is not required without abnormal findings. (Source: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Commence methylphenidate immediate release 10 mg orally twice daily immediately and arrange a follow up review in 4 weeks to assess clinical response and tolerability to the stimulant therapy
options.rationale: Skipping baseline ECG and CV history is below AU standard for stimulant initiation; baseline ECG is recommended in adults before starting any stimulant. (Source: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Refer to cardiology for a stress echocardiogram and 24 hour Holter monitor before any consideration of starting a stimulant medication, due to family history of myocardial infarction in mother at 55
options.rationale: Late-onset MI in a parent (mother >50) does not mandate cardiology workup beyond baseline ECG; this is over-investigation that delays treatment without evidence base. (Source: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Recommend cognitive behavioural therapy alone without medication for the next 6 months given the family history of cardiovascular disease, then reassess for pharmacotherapy after the behavioural trial
options.rationale: Stimulants are first-line pharmacotherapy in adult ADHD with strong evidence; CBT is adjunctive. Family CV history alone does not contraindicate stimulants - baseline screen is enough. (Source: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Trial of atomoxetine 40 mg orally daily without further investigation as a non-stimulant first-line agent that avoids any cardiovascular concerns associated with stimulant medications for adult ADHD
options.rationale: Atomoxetine also requires baseline BP and HR (and has its own CV considerations); the appropriate first step is baseline ECG plus screening, not bypassing stimulants. (Source: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline

explanation.summary: The Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD (AADPA, 2022) recommends, before initiating any stimulant or non-stimulant pharmacotherapy in adults: (1) baseline 12-lead ECG, (2) baseline blood pressure and heart rate, (3) focused personal and family cardiovascular history (syncope, palpitations, exercise intolerance, sudden death <40 years, congenital heart disease, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, long QT), and (4) cardiac examination. Routine cardiology referral is NOT required unless abnormalities are identified. Stimulants are first-line; methylphenidate and dexamfetamine/lisdexamfetamine are accessed via psychiatrist or appropriately authorised paediatrician/GP under state regulations.
key_points: Baseline 12-lead ECG, BP, HR, focused CV history before stimulant.
Routine cardiology referral NOT required without abnormal findings.
Sudden cardiac death family history <40 years is the relevant family screening question.
Monitor BP and HR at 1 month, then 3 monthly on stimulants.
Stimulants are first-line; atomoxetine and guanfacine are second-line.
explanation.pearls: Family history of late MI (parent over 50) does not require cardiology referral before stimulants - the screening question is sudden cardiac death under 40, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, or long QT syndrome.

sources.label: AADPA Adult ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline

subtopic_detail: Borderline PD recurrent self-harm community management
stem:
A 24-year-old woman with a 3-year diagnosis of borderline personality disorder presents to the emergency department for the fourth time this month following superficial cutting of her left forearm. She denies any current suicidal intent. She lives alone in stable accommodation and is engaged with a community case manager and a weekly dialectical behaviour therapy group. Her last admission to hospital was 8 months ago.

On examination she is calm but distressed, asking to be admitted overnight because she does not feel safe at home tonight. The wounds are superficial and have been cleaned and steri-stripped. She has no acute mood or psychotic symptoms. Her case manager is contactable and there is a written safety plan from her DBT therapist in her file. She has no substance intoxication.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84/min, BP 118/76 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Calm but distressed; no psychomotor agitation; no thought disorder; denies current suicidal intent or plan; superficial cuts cleaned and dressed; no active substance intoxication
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods unremarkable; urine drug screen negative; ECG sinus rhythm rate 84; written safety plan from DBT therapist available in file

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition for this patient?

options.text: Voluntary inpatient admission to the acute psychiatric ward for at least 72 hours of observation, milieu support, daily medical review and intensive behavioural and pharmacological intervention monitoring
options.rationale: Repeated brief admissions in BPD without active suicidality, psychosis or severe self-injury are iatrogenic (reinforce help-seeking via crisis, foster dependency); community supports with safety plan review are first-line. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder

options.text: Discharge home with same-day contact to community case manager, safety plan review, DBT crisis skills coaching call within 24 hours, and a planned follow-up DBT group within 72 hours by current therapy provider
options.rationale: NHMRC BPD guideline emphasises community-based crisis support, safety plan review, and DBT skills application rather than admission; ED discharge with rapid community follow-up is the AU evidence-based standard. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder

options.text: Compulsory admission under the Mental Health Act to a closed ward for 7 days observation given the frequency of recurrent ED presentations with deliberate self-harm despite community supports being in place
options.rationale: Compulsory admission requires risk of serious harm AND mental illness amenable to treatment; superficial self-harm in BPD with no current suicidal intent or psychosis does not meet AU Mental Health Act criteria. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder

options.text: Referral to community addictions service for substance use assessment and treatment as the primary intervention rather than ongoing mental health and dialectical behaviour therapy follow up arrangements
options.rationale: There is no evidence of substance use disorder (urine screen negative, no history); inappropriate referral diverts from evidence-based BPD care. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder

options.text: Increase quetiapine to 200 mg orally at night as a sleep and behavioural stabilising medication to reduce her impulsivity, with weekly mental health team review of mood and self-harm behaviour
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy in BPD is symptomatic only and adjunct to psychological treatment; escalating sedative antipsychotic in ED without specialist input is below standard. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder

explanation.summary: The NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder (Project Air) emphasises that admission for self-harm in BPD without active suicidal intent, psychosis, or severe medical injury is iatrogenic and tends to reinforce maladaptive coping. Community-based crisis management with rapid follow-up by the existing care team (case manager, DBT therapist) is the evidence-based AU standard. Brief admissions (24-72 hours) may be considered for safety stabilisation in carefully selected cases but should not be the default. Pharmacotherapy is symptomatic and adjunct to psychological therapy (DBT, MBT, schema therapy, transference-focused psychotherapy). Compulsory admission is reserved for active suicidality with intent and plan plus inability to engage with safety planning.
key_points: Routine admission for BPD self-harm without active suicidality is iatrogenic.
Community-based crisis support with safety plan review is first-line.
DBT is the primary evidence-based psychotherapy.
Pharmacotherapy is symptomatic only; no specific licensed agent.
Compulsory admission requires active suicidality plus inability to safety plan.
explanation.pearls: For BPD self-harm without intent, planned community follow-up within 72 hours and DBT skills coaching outperforms 72-hour observational admissions, which can reinforce help-seeking via crisis.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for Borderline Personality Disorder

subtopic_detail: Alcohol withdrawal in decompensated liver disease - benzodiazepine choice
stem:
A 58-year-old man is admitted to the gastroenterology ward 8 hours ago for management of acute alcohol withdrawal. He drinks approximately 350 g of alcohol per day (35 standard drinks) and has not had a drink for 18 hours. He has known biopsy-proven cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class B) with ascites managed by spironolactone, and is being worked up for liver transplantation.

He is tremulous, diaphoretic, and anxious. He is fully oriented to person, place and time, with no current hallucinations and no seizures. CIWA-Ar score is 22. He has had thiamine 300 mg intravenously already. There is no acute infection. He has not yet had any benzodiazepine. He has had no withdrawal seizures in the past.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 110/min, BP 158/95 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 97% room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Tremulous and diaphoretic; oriented to person/place/time; no hallucinations; no asterixis; abdomen distended with mild ascites; no encephalopathy
data_table.Investigations: INR 1.8 (0.9-1.2), albumin 28 g/L (35-50), bilirubin 45 micromol/L (<20), ALT 80 U/L (<40), platelets 90 x10⁹/L (150-400); ammonia 60 micromol/L (<35); ethanol level negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate benzodiazepine regimen?

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg orally on a symptom triggered regimen every 1 to 2 hours titrated to CIWA-Ar below 8 with target dose ceiling of 80 mg in 24 hours and clinical review thereafter
options.rationale: Diazepam undergoes hepatic oxidation with long-acting active metabolites (desmethyl diazepam); in Child-Pugh B cirrhosis it accumulates causing prolonged sedation and precipitating hepatic encephalopathy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Lorazepam 1 mg orally on a regular regimen with continuous intravenous midazolam infusion as background sedation to control acute autonomic withdrawal symptoms over the first 24 hours
options.rationale: Midazolam infusion is reserved for refractory delirium tremens requiring ICU; first-line is symptom-triggered oral lorazepam or oxazepam; combining with infusion is over-sedation risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Oxazepam 15 to 30 mg orally on a symptom triggered regimen every 1 to 2 hours titrated to CIWA-Ar below 8 with clinical reassessment and downward titration once acute autonomic symptoms settle
options.rationale: Oxazepam undergoes glucuronidation without phase I hepatic oxidation, avoiding accumulation in cirrhosis; symptom-triggered dosing is superior to fixed-schedule in moderate withdrawal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Chlordiazepoxide 50 mg orally every 6 hours on a fixed schedule for 5 days followed by tapering over the next 3 days regardless of clinical course of withdrawal during the first 48 hours
options.rationale: Chlordiazepoxide is hepatically oxidised and accumulates in cirrhosis (similar to diazepam); fixed schedule over-sedates compared with symptom-triggered approach. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Phenobarbital 30 mg orally three times daily as a primary first line agent and arrange referral to addiction medicine for ongoing outpatient relapse prevention follow up after discharge
options.rationale: Phenobarbital is third-line for benzodiazepine-resistant withdrawal in ICU settings; not first-line in any AU outpatient or general ward setting for alcohol withdrawal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic - Alcohol withdrawal)

explanation.summary: Benzodiazepine choice in alcohol withdrawal is dictated by hepatic function. In NORMAL hepatic function, diazepam (long-acting, self-tapering) is first-line. In SIGNIFICANT hepatic impairment (cirrhosis, Child-Pugh B or C), oxazepam, lorazepam, or temazepam (the LOT drugs) are preferred because they are glucuronidated (phase II metabolism) without prior oxidation - they do NOT accumulate in liver disease. Symptom-triggered dosing (CIWA-Ar guided) reduces total benzodiazepine use, length of stay, and incidence of complications versus fixed-schedule regimens. Thiamine (high-dose IV) must be given before any glucose load to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Phenobarbital and continuous infusion benzodiazepines are reserved for benzodiazepine-resistant delirium tremens in ICU.
key_points: Cirrhosis: use oxazepam, lorazepam, or temazepam (LOT) - glucuronidated.
Normal liver: diazepam first-line (long-acting, self-tapering).
Symptom-triggered (CIWA-Ar) reduces total dose and complications.
Parenteral thiamine before any glucose to prevent Wernicke.
Phenobarbital / midazolam infusion: ICU-level refractory withdrawal.
explanation.pearls: LOT drugs (lorazepam, oxazepam, temazepam) are glucuronidated and do not accumulate in cirrhosis - choose oxazepam in any patient with Child-Pugh B or C to avoid encephalopathy from diazepam metabolites.

sources.label: RACP Australian guidelines for the treatment of alcohol problems

subtopic_detail: Refeeding syndrome detection on day 2 of refeed
stem:
A 17-year-old girl has been admitted to the adolescent medicine ward with severe anorexia nervosa. Her BMI is 13.2 kg/m² with 28% weight loss over the past 6 months. She has been eating minimally for the past 4 months. Initial admission bloods: K 3.0 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), Mg 0.65 mmol/L (0.7-1.0), PO4 0.7 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), albumin 30 g/L (35-50). ECG shows QTc 460 ms. Refeeding was commenced 36 hours ago at 1200 kcal/day with thiamine 300 mg IV and oral multivitamin supplementation.

This morning's repeat bloods (day 2 of refeeding) show: K 2.7, Mg 0.55, PO4 0.4. Calcium 2.20 mmol/L (2.10-2.55) corrected. She is asymptomatic with no peripheral oedema, no Chvostek or Trousseau sign, normal cardiac auscultation, and ambulating well. Vital signs are unchanged from admission.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 56/min, BP 95/60 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.2 °C
data_table.Examination: Cachectic; alert, oriented; no peripheral oedema; Chvostek and Trousseau negative; normal cardiac auscultation; weight 36 kg (admission 36 kg)
data_table.Investigations: Day 2 K 2.7 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), Mg 0.55 mmol/L (0.7-1.0), PO4 0.4 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), corrected Ca 2.20 mmol/L (2.10-2.55), albumin 28 g/L (35-50); ECG QTc 470 ms; bedside glucose 4.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate adjustment to her refeeding plan?

options.text: Continue the current 1200 kcal/day caloric target as planned and supplement potassium, phosphate and magnesium orally only, with serial bloods every 24 hours for the next 4 days while on the ward
options.rationale: Continuing same calories with falling electrolytes risks symptomatic refeeding syndrome (cardiac arrhythmia, weakness, encephalopathy); reduction of calories AND IV replacement of phosphate, K and Mg are required. (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders)
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders

options.text: Increase caloric intake to 1800 kcal/day to better restore body weight quickly and add a multivitamin tablet daily, with daily bloods monitored on the adolescent medicine ward for the first week
options.rationale: Increasing calories in falling electrolytes accelerates refeeding syndrome; the principle is to slow caloric advancement when biochemistry deteriorates, not accelerate. (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders)
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders

options.text: Stop refeeding entirely for the next 48 hours and reassess electrolytes and weight before recommencing nutritional support to allow the metabolic disturbance to fully correct prior to nutrition
options.rationale: Stopping refeeding entirely is not appropriate; the principle is to reduce caloric load, replace electrolytes, and continue nutrition. Stopping in severely malnourished patients carries its own risk of further deterioration. (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders)
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders

options.text: Reduce caloric intake to maintenance level, replace phosphate, potassium and magnesium intravenously with thiamine 300 mg IV continued, and monitor electrolytes 6 hourly with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Biochemical refeeding syndrome (falling K, Mg, PO4 within 72 hours of feeding start) mandates calorie reduction, IV electrolyte replacement, continued thiamine, and 6-hourly monitoring with cardiac telemetry until biochemistry stabilises. (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders)
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders

options.text: Transfer the patient to intensive care now and commence total parenteral nutrition with insulin to manage glucose homeostasis during refeeding and intubate for airway protection given her cachexia
options.rationale: ICU and TPN are not first-line; oral or NG refeeding with monitored electrolyte replacement on the adolescent medicine ward is the AU standard for refeeding syndrome. Intubation is unjustified. (Source: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders)
source_refs: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome is a constellation of metabolic disturbances (hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, thiamine deficiency, fluid overload) precipitated by reintroduction of carbohydrate-containing nutrition after prolonged starvation, due to intracellular shifts of phosphate, potassium and magnesium driven by insulin. It occurs within 24-72 hours of refeeding commencement. Detection at the biochemical stage (asymptomatic falling electrolytes) requires slowing of caloric advancement, IV electrolyte replacement, continued high-dose thiamine, and intensive monitoring (6-hourly bloods, continuous ECG). Risk factors: BMI <16, weight loss >15% in 3 months, minimal intake >5 days. Symptomatic refeeding syndrome may cause cardiac arrhythmia, respiratory failure, seizures, rhabdomyolysis, and death.
key_points: Refeeding within 24-72 hours: hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia.
Detection at biochemical stage: reduce calories, IV replacement, continue thiamine.
Continuous ECG monitoring while electrolytes deranged.
Thiamine 300 mg IV before any carb load (Wernicke prevention).
Stopping refeeding entirely is also dangerous in severe malnutrition.
explanation.pearls: Biochemical refeeding syndrome (PO4 dropping below 0.5 on day 2-3) is a slow-the-feed signal - reduce kcal to maintenance, replace PO4/K/Mg IV, keep thiamine going, and monitor every 6 hours.

sources.label: ANZAED Clinical Practice Guideline: Eating disorders
RCH CPG: Eating disorders - inpatient management

subtopic_detail: Postpartum psychosis with infant-directed command hallucinations
stem:
A 31-year-old G1P1 woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband on day 6 postpartum because she is making no sense and has not slept for 3 days. She believes the baby is dead inside and that she must send him back. She has had a previous diagnosis of bipolar I disorder with manic-only episodes, well controlled on lithium 750 mg nocte which she ceased 6 months prior to conception under specialist advice.

The baby is currently being cared for by the patient's mother at home and is well. The patient is currently exclusively breastfeeding. On mental state examination she is agitated with disorganised speech, with auditory hallucinations telling her to send the baby back, and persecutory delusions about hospital staff. There are no focal neurological signs and no signs of sepsis.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 105/min, BP 130/82 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Agitated, disorganised, command auditory hallucinations directed at infant; persecutory delusions; no focal neurology; reflexes symmetric; no neck stiffness; perineum healing well
data_table.Investigations: FBC, electrolytes, calcium, magnesium, glucose, LFTs within normal limits; TSH 1.8 mIU/L (0.4-4.0); urine drug screen negative; B12 and folate within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg orally now and discharge home with husband supervision, with outpatient psychiatry review within 24 hours and community mental health team contact within the working day
options.rationale: Command hallucinations directed at infant in postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency with high infanticide risk; discharge home with supervision is inadequate. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Recommence sertraline 50 mg orally daily as postpartum depression treatment with weekly community mental health team review for ongoing assessment and supportive management in the home environment
options.rationale: This is postpartum psychosis (psychotic features, mood-incongruent delusions, infant-directed harm risk), not postpartum depression; SSRI alone is inadequate and delays antipsychotic treatment. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Same-day electroconvulsive therapy in the emergency department with brief consent process before any transfer to inpatient psychiatric care or mother baby unit elsewhere in the regional health network
options.rationale: ECT may have a role in refractory or severe puerperal psychosis but is not the immediate ED first step; admission with antipsychotic and lithium reinstatement first-line, ECT if response inadequate. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Compulsory admission to a general medical ward for overnight observation, pending formal psychiatry review in the morning, with husband requested to remain at bedside throughout for safety and support
options.rationale: General medical ward lacks psychiatric monitoring, security, and infant access; admission to psychiatric inpatient unit (ideally Mother-Baby Unit) is the AU standard. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

options.text: Involuntary admission to a Mother-Baby Unit with olanzapine, lithium reintroduction with breastfeeding cessation, and ECT consideration if response is inadequate within 48 to 72 hours of admission
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis with command hallucinations re infant requires involuntary admission; MBU preserves mother-infant attachment where capacity allows; olanzapine plus lithium reintroduction (lithium contraindicated in breastfeeding so cease) is AU first-line; ECT for inadequate response. (Source: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period)
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis (puerperal psychosis) is a rare (1 in 1000 births) but severe psychiatric emergency presenting within 2 weeks of delivery with mood symptoms (most commonly mania, sometimes mixed or depressive), psychotic features, disorganisation and confusion. Women with a history of bipolar I disorder (especially those who discontinue mood stabilisers preconception) are at the highest risk - approximately 1 in 4 will develop puerperal psychosis. Infanticide and suicide risk are high. Management is involuntary admission to a Mother-Baby Unit (or general psychiatric inpatient unit with infant access where MBU unavailable), antipsychotic (olanzapine is well-studied in postpartum), reinstatement of lithium (which is incompatible with breastfeeding, so support cessation), and consideration of ECT for rapid response in severe cases. Long-term plan includes mood stabiliser prophylaxis through any future pregnancies.
key_points: Onset within 2 weeks postpartum; mania most common syndrome.
Bipolar I women off mood stabilisers preconception have ~25% risk.
Involuntary admission to MBU (or adult psych unit) for safety and treatment.
Olanzapine first-line acute; lithium reintroduction once breastfeeding ceased.
ECT considered for severe or refractory presentations within 48-72 hours.
explanation.pearls: Bipolar I women who stop lithium before conception carry a 1 in 4 risk of postpartum psychosis - the priority on representation is MBU admission, antipsychotic, and lithium reintroduction with breastfeeding cessation.

sources.label: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period
RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

subtopic_detail: Submassive (intermediate-risk) pulmonary embolism management
stem:
A 64-year-old man is reviewed in the orthopaedic ward 3 days after right total knee replacement. He has been on enoxaparin 40 mg daily as prophylaxis. He develops sudden dyspnoea and pleuritic right-sided chest pain over the past 30 minutes. He has no history of bleeding, no recent stroke, and no relevant family history.

On examination he is alert and oriented. He has tachypnoea, oxygen saturations of 88% on room air improving to 95% on 4 L by nasal prong. There are no signs of haemodynamic shock. The CTPA performed urgently shows a large saddle embolus with right ventricular dilation and a right-to-left ventricle diameter ratio greater than 1.0. There is no signs of overt cardiogenic shock and he remains awake and conversing throughout assessment.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118/min, BP 105/72 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 95% on 4 L nasal prong, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert; mild respiratory distress; clear chest auscultation; cardiac dual heart sounds no murmur; right calf swelling 2 cm greater than left circumference; no overt cardiogenic shock signs
data_table.Investigations: Troponin I 95 ng/L (<14), BNP 850 pg/mL (<100); CTPA large saddle PE with RV dilation, RV:LV ratio >1.0; eGFR 55 mL/min/1.73 m²; INR 1.0, platelets 195 x10⁹/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous unfractionated heparin infusion with HDU admission for close haemodynamic monitoring and cardiology and respiratory input, reserving systemic thrombolysis for haemodynamic deterioration
options.rationale: Intermediate-risk (submassive) PE with normal BP is treated with anticoagulation alone and close monitoring; systemic thrombolysis is reserved for haemodynamic decompensation (PEITHO showed thrombolysis prevents deterioration but increases major bleeding). UFH preferred over LMWH in renal impairment and possible bleeding need. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute PE position statement)

options.text: Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg infused over 2 hours immediately given the size of the saddle embolus and right ventricular dilation seen on the CT pulmonary angiogram performed in radiology
options.rationale: Routine thrombolysis in intermediate-risk PE (without shock) increases major bleeding (PEITHO) without improving mortality; reserved for haemodynamic deterioration on heparin. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute PE position statement)

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin 1 mg per kg subcutaneously twice daily and ward admission for ongoing observation and serial troponin and BNP monitoring while continuing standard nursing observations
options.rationale: LMWH at therapeutic dose is acceptable in low-risk PE but in intermediate-risk PE with renal impairment and possible need to switch to thrombolysis, IV UFH (rapidly reversible) is preferred in HDU. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute PE position statement)

options.text: Catheter-directed mechanical thrombectomy as definitive first-line therapy in the cath lab today, followed by therapeutic warfarin from day 1 with bridging heparin until INR is therapeutic over the next week
options.rationale: Catheter-directed therapies are reserved for high-risk PE with shock or contraindication to thrombolysis; not first-line in intermediate-risk PE. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute PE position statement)

options.text: Insertion of inferior vena cava filter alone without anticoagulation given the recent surgery and possible bleeding concerns, with reassessment for anticoagulation start in 7 days postoperatively
options.rationale: IVC filter without anticoagulation in established PE has no role unless absolute contraindication to anticoagulation; this patient has none, and bleeding risk from a knee replacement at day 3 is acceptable for UFH. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute PE position statement)

explanation.summary: Pulmonary embolism is risk-stratified into high (shock or sustained hypotension), intermediate (RV dysfunction by imaging or biomarkers without shock), and low (no RV dysfunction, no biomarker rise) categories. Intermediate-risk PE is further divided into intermediate-high (both imaging RV dysfunction AND biomarker rise) and intermediate-low (one or other). Anticoagulation with IV UFH or LMWH or DOAC is the foundation; systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100 mg over 2 h or weight-based 0.6 mg/kg) is RESERVED for high-risk PE (with shock) or intermediate-risk PE that deteriorates haemodynamically on anticoagulation. The PEITHO trial showed routine thrombolysis in intermediate-risk PE prevented haemodynamic decompensation but doubled major bleeding without mortality benefit. Catheter-directed therapies are an alternative when systemic thrombolysis is contraindicated or fails.
key_points: Risk stratify: high (shock), intermediate (RV dysfunction), low.
Anticoagulation foundation: UFH, LMWH, or DOAC.
Systemic thrombolysis reserved for high-risk PE or intermediate that decompensates.
PEITHO: routine thrombolysis in intermediate-risk doubles major bleeding.
UFH preferred when bleeding risk uncertain (reversible with protamine).
explanation.pearls: PEITHO trial: routine alteplase in intermediate-risk PE doubles major bleeding without reducing mortality - reserve thrombolysis for the patient who actually decompensates on heparin.

sources.label: ESC Guidelines for Acute Pulmonary Embolism 2019

subtopic_detail: Adult DKA - fluid first then insulin with potassium-led delay
stem:
A 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed age 11) presents to the emergency department with 12 hours of vomiting, polyuria, polydipsia and abdominal pain following a gastrointestinal viral illness. Her insulin pump dislodged 48 hours ago and she has not replaced the cannula. She weighs 65 kg.

On examination she is alert with Kussmaul respiration. Her mucous membranes are dry and capillary refill is 3 seconds. There is no focal abdominal tenderness or peritonism. Her bedside glucose is 28 mmol/L with serum ketones of 5.8 mmol/L. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.10, HCO3 10 mmol/L. Serum potassium is 5.4 mmol/L. She is haemodynamically compensated. She is fully oriented and her speech is coherent throughout the assessment.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 95/60 mmHg, RR 28/min with Kussmaul respiration, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert and oriented; Kussmaul respiration; dry mucous membranes; capillary refill 3 seconds; soft abdomen with mild diffuse tenderness; no peritonism; pump site erythematous
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 28 mmol/L (4-7); ketones 5.8 mmol/L (<0.6); pH 7.10, HCO3 10 mmol/L (22-26), anion gap 24; Na 134 mmol/L (135-145), K 5.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 110 micromol/L (45-90)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Commence intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 unit per kilogram per hour immediately as the first intervention and add intravenous fluid resuscitation simultaneously as bicarbonate measurement results come back
options.rationale: Insulin before fluid resuscitation in DKA can precipitate cardiovascular collapse and worsen intracellular potassium shift; fluid is started first per Australian DKA guidelines. (Source: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management)
source_refs: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management

options.text: Commence sodium chloride 0.9% 1 L over 1 hour as initial fluid resuscitation, then add intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 unit per kilogram per hour, with hourly potassium monitoring and replacement once below 5.5
options.rationale: AU adult DKA protocol: 1 L 0.9% saline over 1 h first, then insulin infusion 0.1 U/kg/h; K replacement only when serum K <5.5 (high risk of arrhythmia from hypokalaemia as insulin drives K intracellularly). (Source: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management)
source_refs: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management

options.text: Administer a bicarbonate infusion 100 mmol over 1 hour to correct the acidosis as the priority first intervention, followed by fluid resuscitation and insulin therapy once the pH normalises
options.rationale: Bicarbonate in DKA does not improve outcomes and may worsen hypokalaemia and paradoxical CSF acidosis; reserved for pH <6.9 only. (Source: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management)
source_refs: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management

options.text: Commence 10% dextrose infusion with subcutaneous intermediate acting insulin twice daily to address dehydration and provide background insulin while continuing oral rehydration with dietary salt
options.rationale: Dextrose is added only when glucose falls below 14 mmol/L during DKA treatment; SC intermediate insulin is not appropriate for acute DKA management. (Source: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management)
source_refs: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management

options.text: Trial of oral rehydration solution with subcutaneous rapid acting insulin 0.2 unit per kilogram and review in 4 hours given haemodynamic stability and ability to tolerate oral intake during this presentation
options.rationale: Oral rehydration and SC insulin alone is inadequate for established DKA with pH 7.10 and significant ketonaemia; IV protocol is required. (Source: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management)
source_refs: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management

explanation.summary: Adult diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) is defined by hyperglycaemia (>11 mmol/L), ketonaemia (>3 mmol/L) or ketonuria, and metabolic acidosis (pH <7.30 or HCO3 <15). Management has 4 pillars: (1) fluid first - 1 L 0.9% sodium chloride over 1 h, then 1 L over 2 h, then 1 L over 4 h with adjustments for haemodynamic state; (2) insulin infusion at 0.1 U/kg/h started AFTER first fluid bag; (3) potassium replacement guided by serum K - K <3.3 hold insulin and replace K first, K 3.3-5.5 add KCl to fluid (40 mmol/L), K >5.5 no replacement; (4) dextrose added when glucose <14 mmol/L. Bicarbonate only if pH <6.9. Hourly bloods until anion gap resolves. Look for precipitant (infection, pump failure, non-adherence, MI). Transition to subcut insulin only when anion gap resolved and patient eating.
key_points: Fluid first (1 L 0.9% saline over 1 h), then insulin infusion 0.1 U/kg/h.
K <3.3 hold insulin; 3.3-5.5 replace; >5.5 no replacement.
Add 10% dextrose when glucose <14 mmol/L.
Bicarbonate only for pH <6.9.
Look for precipitant: infection, pump failure, non-adherence, MI.
explanation.pearls: In adult DKA, fluid comes first, insulin second, and potassium replacement waits until serum K drops below 5.5 - the insulin infusion will drop K within 2 hours.

sources.label: ADIPS / RACP Diabetes Australia: Adult DKA Management

subtopic_detail: NSTEMI high GRACE score - early invasive within 24 hours
stem:
A 68-year-old man with hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus and ex-smoker (20 pack-years) presents to the emergency department 4 hours after an episode of substernal chest discomfort lasting 30 minutes at rest, now resolved. He drove himself to hospital. He has no prior history of acute coronary syndrome and is on metformin, perindopril and atorvastatin.

On examination he is comfortable and pain free. There are no signs of heart failure or new murmur. His ECG on arrival shows 1 mm of ST depression in leads V4 to V6 with T wave inversion in I, aVL, V5 and V6. Troponin I drawn on arrival is 65 ng/L; repeat at 1 hour is 95 ng/L. His GRACE risk score in ED is 145. There is no history of bleeding and his estimated GFR is 65 mL/min/1.73 m².

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88/min, BP 135/85 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Comfortable, pain free; no signs of heart failure; cardiac dual heart sounds no murmur; chest auscultation clear; peripheral pulses normal; no signs of bleeding diathesis
data_table.Investigations: Troponin I 0 h 65 ng/L (<14), 1 h 95 ng/L; ECG ST depression V4-V6 plus T-wave inversion I/aVL/V5-V6; eGFR 65 mL/min/1.73 m²; haemoglobin 138 g/L (130-180); HbA1c 8.0%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Conservative ischaemia-guided management with discharge after 24 hours of telemetry observation if asymptomatic and serial troponins down trending, with outpatient stress test arranged within 2 weeks
options.rationale: GRACE score >140 categorises as high risk; conservative ischaemia-guided strategy is for low-risk NSTEMI; high risk warrants early invasive within 24 hours. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline)

options.text: Immediate primary percutaneous coronary intervention within 90 minutes of arrival as if this were ST elevation myocardial infarction given the troponin rise and chest pain history at rest in this patient
options.rationale: Primary PCI within 90 min is for STEMI (or NSTE-ACS with refractory chest pain, electrical/haemodynamic instability, mechanical complication, or VT/VF). NSTEMI with high GRACE without these features is invasive within 24 hours. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline)

options.text: Aspirin 300 mg loading dose plus ticagrelor 180 mg loading dose plus fondaparinux 2.5 mg subcutaneously plus cardiology admission with invasive coronary angiography within 24 hours of arrival
options.rationale: GRACE >140 = high-risk NSTEMI; AU protocol is dual antiplatelet loading (aspirin 300 mg + ticagrelor 180 mg) plus parenteral anticoagulant (fondaparinux preferred over enoxaparin in stable AU practice unless undergoing immediate PCI) and invasive angiography within 24 hours. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline)

options.text: Thrombolysis with tenecteplase weight adjusted intravenous bolus dose given immediately as if this were ST elevation infarction, with subsequent transfer to a tertiary cardiology centre for ongoing care
options.rationale: Thrombolysis is contraindicated in NSTE-ACS (no transmural occlusion to lyse); inappropriate and would increase bleeding without benefit. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline)

options.text: Outpatient CT coronary angiography in 4 weeks with cardiology clinic review, with discharge today on aspirin only given the chest pain has resolved and ECG changes are non-specific in their distribution
options.rationale: Discharge of a high-risk NSTEMI (GRACE >140) for outpatient CT-CA risks recurrent infarction and death; inpatient invasive workup is mandatory. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ ACS Guideline)

explanation.summary: NSTE-ACS is risk-stratified by GRACE score (or TIMI) into very high, high, intermediate, and low risk. Invasive timing: VERY HIGH risk (haemodynamic instability, refractory chest pain, life-threatening arrhythmia, mechanical complication) - immediate angiography (<2 hours). HIGH risk (GRACE >140, dynamic ST/T changes, troponin rise) - early invasive (<24 hours). INTERMEDIATE risk (diabetes, renal dysfunction, EF <40%, prior PCI/CABG, GRACE 109-140) - delayed invasive (<72 hours). LOW risk - ischaemia-guided strategy with non-invasive testing. All NSTE-ACS get dual antiplatelet loading (aspirin + ticagrelor or prasugrel or clopidogrel), parenteral anticoagulation (fondaparinux for non-immediate PCI, UFH or enoxaparin for PCI), statin, beta-blocker, ACEI.
key_points: GRACE >140 = high-risk NSTEMI, early invasive within 24 hours.
Aspirin 300 mg + ticagrelor 180 mg dual antiplatelet loading.
Fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC preferred when PCI not immediate.
Thrombolysis CONTRAINDICATED in NSTE-ACS (no transmural occlusion).
Very high risk (instability) needs immediate angiography <2 hours.
explanation.pearls: GRACE over 140 is the 24 hour gate to the cath lab for NSTEMI - ticagrelor 180 mg load is preferred over clopidogrel unless oral anticoagulant on board or high bleeding risk.

subtopic_detail: Tumour lysis syndrome - rasburicase and hypocalcaemia management
stem:
A 32-year-old man newly diagnosed with Burkitt lymphoma was admitted 36 hours ago to commence chemotherapy. Pre-chemotherapy bloods (baseline) were: K 4.0 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), urate 480 micromol/L (200-420), phosphate 1.4 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), calcium 2.30 mmol/L (2.10-2.55) corrected, creatinine 95 micromol/L (45-90), LDH 1200 U/L (<250), estimated GFR 75 mL/min/1.73 m². He was started on allopurinol 300 mg orally daily and IV maintenance fluids 24 hours before chemotherapy.

12 hours after first dose of rituximab plus cyclophosphamide: K 6.1 mmol/L, urate 720 micromol/L, phosphate 2.1 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), corrected calcium 1.85 mmol/L, creatinine 145 micromol/L, LDH 2400 U/L. Urine output is 30 mL/h. ECG shows no peaked T waves and no QRS widening. G6PD assay sent 24 hours ago is still pending. He is asymptomatic.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92/min, BP 130/80 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, comfortable; no Chvostek or Trousseau; no arrhythmia on monitor; no peripheral oedema; urine output 30 mL/h over past 4 hours
data_table.Investigations: K 6.1 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), urate 720 micromol/L (200-420), phosphate 2.1 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), corrected calcium 1.85 mmol/L (2.10-2.55), creatinine 145 micromol/L (45-90), LDH 2400 U/L (<250); G6PD assay pending; ECG no peaked T or QRS widening

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Maintenance fluids and oral allopurinol 300 mg daily with biochemistry recheck after 24 hours of chemotherapy completion and dietary phosphate restriction overnight on the haematology ward
options.rationale: Maintenance fluids alone are inadequate in established TLS (Cairo-Bishop criteria met); rasburicase, aggressive hydration to UO 100 mL/h, and dialysis criteria review are required. (Source: HSANZ TLS prevention and management)

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% to correct hypocalcaemia immediately with continued intravenous maintenance fluids and oral allopurinol therapy and recheck in 6 hours overnight
options.rationale: Asymptomatic hypocalcaemia in TLS should NOT be corrected with calcium - it precipitates as calcium phosphate causing AKI and worsens metastatic calcification; treat the hyperphosphataemia instead. (Source: HSANZ TLS prevention and management)

options.text: Sodium bicarbonate infusion to alkalinise urine and oral allopurinol 600 mg daily with maintenance fluids and dietary potassium restriction to prevent further potassium rise overnight on the ward
options.rationale: Urinary alkalinisation is no longer recommended in TLS - it worsens calcium phosphate precipitation when phosphate is high; rasburicase is the AU first-line in established TLS. (Source: HSANZ TLS prevention and management)

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride targeting urine output 100 mL per hour, single dose rasburicase 0.2 mg per kg IV, frequent biochemistry, and renal-replacement therapy if refractory to medical management
options.rationale: Established TLS in high-risk haematological malignancy: aggressive hydration (UO 100 mL/h), rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) drops urate within hours; dialysis for refractory hyperkalaemia, oliguria, or fluid overload. G6PD result not required to give first dose if otherwise indicated and the patient is not in a known G6PD-deficient demographic. (Source: HSANZ TLS prevention and management)

options.text: Withhold rasburicase pending G6PD assay result, continue allopurinol 600 mg daily, start sodium bicarbonate infusion to aid uric acid clearance and continue regular monitoring overnight on the haematology ward
options.rationale: Waiting for G6PD result delays critical urate lowering; in emergent TLS, give first dose of rasburicase and accept the small risk if G6PD-deficient (haemolysis), particularly in non-African/Mediterranean ancestry; alkalinisation harms calcium-phosphate handling. (Source: HSANZ TLS prevention and management)

explanation.summary: Tumour lysis syndrome is defined by Cairo-Bishop criteria: any 2 of K >6.0, urate >476 micromol/L, phosphate >1.45 mmol/L, calcium <1.75 mmol/L, OR a 25% change from baseline, within 3 days before or 7 days after chemotherapy commencement. CLINICAL TLS adds creatinine >1.5x ULN, arrhythmia, seizure, or sudden death. High-risk malignancies: Burkitt lymphoma, T-cell ALL, blastic phase CML, AML with high WBC. Prevention in high-risk: rasburicase prophylaxis, aggressive hydration. Established TLS: aggressive IV hydration targeting UO 100 mL/h, rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV (drops urate within hours by enzymatic oxidation to soluble allantoin), correction of hyperkalaemia (calcium gluconate, insulin/dextrose, salbutamol), do NOT correct asymptomatic hypocalcaemia (causes calcium phosphate precipitation), urinary alkalinisation NO LONGER recommended. Dialysis for refractory hyperkalaemia, oliguria, or fluid overload.
key_points: Cairo-Bishop: any 2 of K/urate/PO4/Ca derangement.
Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV - drops urate within hours.
Do NOT correct asymptomatic hypocalcaemia (precipitates calcium phosphate).
Urinary alkalinisation no longer recommended (worsens Ca-PO4 precipitation).
Dialysis for refractory hyperK, oliguria, or fluid overload.
explanation.pearls: Asymptomatic hypocalcaemia in TLS gets the phosphate treated, not the calcium - injecting calcium gluconate into a high-phosphate state precipitates calcium phosphate in the kidneys and worsens the AKI.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Tumour lysis syndrome

subtopic_detail: Cushing syndrome - 2-of-3 first-line biochemical screening
stem:
A 41-year-old woman is seen in the endocrine outpatient clinic with an 18-month history of progressive central weight gain (12 kg), facial fullness with persistent facial redness, easy bruising on her forearms, oligomenorrhoea, and proximal muscle weakness with difficulty climbing stairs. Two months ago she was newly diagnosed with hypertension (BP 165/100) and type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.2%). She is on no glucocorticoids and has no exogenous steroid exposure including inhaled, topical, or intra-articular.

On examination she has wide (2 cm) purple abdominal striae, supraclavicular fullness, and proximal muscle weakness (Medical Research Council grade 4/5 hip flexion bilaterally). Her potassium is 3.1 mmol/L (3.5-5.0) and her random morning serum cortisol is 920 nmol/L (50-550 morning). She has not yet had any other endocrine investigations.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88/min, BP 165/100 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Cushingoid appearance; central obesity, facial fullness with plethora; supraclavicular fullness; wide purple abdominal striae 2 cm; proximal weakness MRC 4 of 5 hip flexion bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: K 3.1 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), HbA1c 8.2%, random AM cortisol 920 nmol/L (morning 50-550); FBC, LFTs, TSH within normal limits; pregnancy test negative; no exogenous steroid exposure

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line investigation strategy?

options.text: Pituitary MRI with contrast immediately as the first investigation, then proceed to biochemical workup only if a pituitary mass is identified on the imaging study and refer to neurosurgery
options.rationale: Pituitary incidentalomas occur in 10-20%; imaging before biochemical confirmation cannot distinguish hormone-secreting from non-secreting lesions. Biochemistry first to confirm hypercortisolism is the AU standard. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing Syndrome Guideline)

options.text: Short Synacthen test 250 mcg with serum cortisol at baseline 30 minutes and 60 minutes to assess adrenal cortisol production response capacity over the standard testing interval used clinically
options.rationale: Short Synacthen tests for adrenal INsufficiency (Addison's), not Cushing syndrome (hypercortisolism); inappropriate test for the clinical question. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing Syndrome Guideline)

options.text: Random serum ACTH alone with serum cortisol at the time of sampling, taken at a single time point in the morning of clinic review, for interpretation against a normal range provided by the laboratory
options.rationale: Random ACTH alone cannot diagnose or differentiate Cushing syndrome aetiologies; it has a role AFTER hypercortisolism is biochemically confirmed to differentiate ACTH-dependent vs independent disease. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing Syndrome Guideline)

options.text: Plasma renin to aldosterone ratio and saline suppression test for primary hyperaldosteronism workup as the priority investigation given the hypertension and hypokalaemia features that have developed recently
options.rationale: Renin-aldosterone workup investigates Conn's syndrome; the clinical phenotype here (wide striae, central obesity, proximal weakness, glucose intolerance) is Cushing syndrome and demands cortisol-axis workup. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing Syndrome Guideline)

options.text: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test and 24 hour urinary free cortisol as first-line screening, with plasma ACTH measurement once two of three screening tests confirm hypercortisolism over the next week
options.rationale: Endocrine Society first-line: any 2 of 3 (1 mg overnight DST, 24 h UFC, late-night salivary cortisol) positive to confirm hypercortisolism; ACTH then differentiates ACTH-dependent (pituitary, ectopic) vs ACTH-independent (adrenal) disease. (Source: Endocrine Society Cushing Syndrome Guideline)

explanation.summary: Cushing syndrome workup proceeds in 3 stages: (1) BIOCHEMICAL CONFIRMATION of hypercortisolism using ANY 2 of 3 first-line tests (1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol); single test has insufficient specificity. (2) ACTH-DEPENDENT vs ACTH-INDEPENDENT differentiation by plasma ACTH (low <2 pmol/L = adrenal cause, normal/high = pituitary or ectopic). (3) LOCALISATION by pituitary MRI (Cushing disease), inferior petrosal sinus sampling if discordant, CT chest/abdomen for ectopic ACTH or adrenal mass. Pseudo-Cushing causes (obesity, depression, alcoholism, pregnancy) may give false positives; exogenous steroid use is the most common false positive cause overall. Skipping straight to imaging causes misdiagnosis from incidentalomas.
key_points: Confirm hypercortisolism FIRST with 2 of 3: DST, UFC, salivary cortisol.
Then ACTH to differentiate ACTH-dependent vs independent.
Pituitary MRI for ACTH-dependent; CT abdomen for ACTH-independent.
Inferior petrosal sinus sampling if pituitary MRI normal but ACTH-dependent.
Exclude exogenous steroid use as the most common false positive cause.
explanation.pearls: 2-of-3 screening (1 mg DST, 24 h UFC, late-night salivary cortisol) confirms Cushing biochemically before any imaging - pituitary incidentalomas in 10-20% mean MRI-first invites misdiagnosis.
